My engineering dork hack? A hybrid of Wunsch's Case grounded [DC transformer](https://www.amazon.com/Meanwell-Enclosed-Approved-Switching-Transformer/dp/B00GPWLVHI/ref=sr_1_3?ie=UTF8&qid=1502553581&sr=8-3&keywords=meanwell+ac+to+dc+transformer) far away (especially not directly under my colon or junk) and a [dc incandescent bulb](https://www.amazon.com/s/ref=nb_sb_noss?url=search-alias%3Dtools&field-keywords=dc+incaandescent&rh=n%3A228013%2Ck%3Adc+incaandescent) or two maybe throw in some a 12v [UV led strip](https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B06XWCS4KB/ref=oh_aui_detailpage_o03_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1). The hack I'd throw in is a nice fat capacitor to further dimish the ripple voltage that ALL AC/DC bridge rectifiers produce. Being outside is way better though.

# Thread: raising body temperature by exercising in the morning is effective for resetting circadian rhythms?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ValerieBee — 2017-08-08T21:13:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-221205>

I thought I read or heard @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) recommend not to exercise right before or after sunrise? I can't remember where, and I've been wondering about this. I did see in [Blog CT7](https://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/) it appears mid-afternoon is prime time physically (funny enough, that is the time I know personally my brain seems to shut off, and my body wants to be active.)

Also does that statement make sense? ( [I happened across it](https://blog.bulletproof.com/does-grounding-work/) reading up on earthing.) Am I on the right path that the SCN is the mechanism of the body clock, meaning circadian rhythm? Is the tail wagging the dog on using temperature to change biorhythm, or what am I missing? I do see a bit of research that correlates body temp and circadian rhythm (and menstrual cycles), but of course correlation is not causation.

### kris90 — 2017-08-08T21:57:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-221208>

I train first thing in the morning 2-3x per week with heavy weights. Because I am up so early before sunrise, I use cold as my stimulus, so after waking, and going outside to take a few deep breaths of fresh air, I hop in a cold shower, and it wakes me right up. Then I do some stretching and warm up, and onto the heavy weights. I end up getting my sunrise after my workout.

Haven't noticed any issues with this. I don't eat post workout either, usually push my first meal until around noon, and just drink lots of cold water. I like getting my work out done with in the morning, and I find it stimulates me for the day.

TBH, and this is just my opinion/experience, when you are leptin sensitive, I think you can program your circadian rhythms accordingly, as long as you keep them between sunrise and sunset. Ideally, I would like to exercise after sunrise, and I do on weekends, but with my damn job/schedule, I don't have this, so I have to biohack it, and find the cold works wonders for waking me up those early mornings.

### Penny — 2017-08-08T22:21:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-221211>

> [kris90 said:](/goto/post?id=221208)
>
> I train first thing in the morning 2-3x per week with heavy weights. Because I am up so early before sunrise, I use cold as my stimulus, so after waking, and going outside to take a few deep breaths of fresh air, I hop in a cold shower, and it wakes me right up. Then I do some stretching and warm up, and onto the heavy weights. I end up getting my sunrise after my workout.
>
> Haven't noticed any issues with this. I don't eat post workout either, usually push my first meal until around noon, and just drink lots of cold water. I like getting my work out done with in the morning, and I find it stimulates me for the day.
>
> TBH, and this is just my opinion/experience, when you are leptin sensitive, I think you can program your circadian rhythms accordingly, as long as you keep them between sunrise and sunset. Ideally, I would like to exercise after sunrise, and I do on weekends, but with my damn job/schedule, I don't have this, so I have to biohack it, and find the cold works wonders for waking me up those early mornings.

I think it's because when cortisol spikes in the morning, it uses up electrons - so if you exercise at that time, maybe you're doing it with a half empty gas tank? But, in someone healthy, it's probably not a big deal - I used to swim at dawn outside and it was fine, but swimming indoors, I think it made me tired... I would go with what feels good - I also think this is why people have heart attacks in the morning - less electrons -

### kris90 — 2017-08-08T23:44:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-221220>

Ya that makes a lot of sense. Ideally, on a day I don't have to work, I still enjoy my morning workouts, but I ease into them a little more by atleast engaging in them post-sunrise, and maybe a coffee beforehand.

That's why on days I cannot workout a little later, I'll use cold as my stimulus. Seems to work alright.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-08-08T23:46:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-221221>

Exercise makes a mitochondrion release heat or red light. We don't have spectral charts on mitochondrial haplotypes and what they do to water in cells made by mitochondria. This is critical data in aquaphotomics. In fact, most red light therapy devices don’t publish their output intensity, which makes it nearly impossible to determine how much energy is being delivered to the treatment area. This is key to understanding is water energized to being a mirror, and engine, or a battery in tissue. #redlightfacts

### JohnnyAZ — 2017-08-22T08:31:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-222027>

Before I found this site and I was blindly dealing with my issues I regularly worked out early in the AM. I did a cortisol spit test and when I did so I did one vial right after waking and my level was ok...not good...but ok. After I worked out I didn't feel well and did another vial early to see if my cortisol was affected. When the results came back the level on my post-workout had a drastically low cortisol level. It was below what it should have been at 9pm (based on the graph presented in the results). Since then I now workout later (based on Jack's advice in blog, forum, and webinars) and I do it from home where I can keep walking out into the sun and stand barefoot on the ground in between each set instead of in a gym under the blue lights. I don't have results on this as my most recent cortisol test was just sent in today but I do feel better throughout the day.

### kris90 — 2017-08-23T16:59:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-222089>

I've noticed my basal body temperature has now dropped down to between 36.0 and 36.5. I feel lizard like, because my cold receptors are almost always activated (i.e. goosebumps & vasoconstriction) unless I bask in the sun, at which point vasodilation occurs very rapidly.

Yesterday, it was very hot and humid outside (35 C with the humidity). But because it was overcast and rainy, I still had goosebumps and vasoconstriction outside. My guess is that most of the UV light was blocked by cloud coverage.

The only other factor that reverses goosebumps and vasoconstriction is eating. And that makes sense giving that eating food is simply delivering light frequencies to the respiratory proteins. With the minimal clothing I wear, people think I'm warm all the time, but it's actually the opposite. I'm always cold until I have UV/IR light in my environment or through food. I've just learned to love feeling cold. Wim Hof breathing can be used to decrease feelings of cold by increasing oxygen uptake/tunneling more electrons through ETC and releasing more IR light in my mitochondria.

### drezy — 2017-08-23T19:03:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-222097>

@[kris90](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19157/) what is your exercise approach going to be when the UV yield goes down approaching winter solstice? I figure that you've got a plan.

### kris90 — 2017-08-23T20:08:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-222099>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=222097)
>
> @[kris90](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19157/) what is your exercise approach going to be when the UV yield goes down approaching winter solstice? I figure that you've got a plan.

I'm already shifting things a little. Working on a plan to telecommute more this winter, to relieve stress of waking up so early and having to drive into work in darkness. I will use that day to exercise on my lunch, shifting my workout to early PM from early AM.

Also, cutting back training to 2 days per week. When I'm less charged by UV light, exercise becomes more detrimental. I do plan to biohack with blacklights though, as well as using a tanning bed. It did me very well last winter.

### drezy — 2017-08-23T20:14:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-222101>

Good. I want you around for the longhaul for your two babies.

**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-listen-to-this-interview.19986/>

**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2017-08-10T04:42:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-listen-to-this-interview.19986/post-221302>

[http://thewellnesscouch.com/tps/tps-215-the-paleo-lifestyle-for-mental-health](http://thewellnesscouch.com/tps/tps-215-the-paleo-lifestyle-for-mental-health)

Michelle has been a member of this forum for a long time.....

Please have a listen and hear what she is up to.

This is so inspirational ....she is making such a difference

Any thoughts on what she is doing? I suspect she needs all the support she can get.....

### drezy — 2017-08-10T05:39:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-listen-to-this-interview.19986/post-221305>

I admire her and have became an instant fan.

### caroline — 2017-08-10T05:45:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-listen-to-this-interview.19986/post-221306>

I don't know how Michelle is bankrolling all this - But Tony and I decided to help a little.

Tony actually sat and listened to her whole interview and found it very inspirational. We can't wait to pop over on our travels!

### Da-mo — 2017-08-10T08:36:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-listen-to-this-interview.19986/post-221313>

Michelle mentions people who struggle with anxiety but dont have addiction issues - that is my 23 year old daughter.

I know what she needs but as with other things Dad knows nothing until she crashes and burns and then she might listen to me.

The system treats her with drugs and counselling when what she really needs is to control her light and nnEMF environment. She has a distance relationship with a lovely young man in Tulsa which unfortunately means skyping and video comms late at night for the both of them.

My next move here is to install a wired LAN and ditch the WLAN altogether. Next move is to send some Uvex blue blockers to her significant other and give her a pair for them both to wear when vid-calling. I will ask him to wear them for her sake - I'm sure he will be on board.

I just dropped her at the airport today to go see him - unfortunately for her that means 3 months (VISA limits) living in the city of Tulsa in an apartment and all the nnEMF that goes with it.

Michelle makes a good point about the socioeconomic system - if you are at the bottom and are an addict there is money to throw at you. If you are at the top you can afford the expertise (assuming you know how to find the right experts) but if you are somewhere in the middle you are completely on your own. Thank goodness there are the likes of JK to help us but unfortunately not everyone will find him and, of those, not everyone will have the dopamine to stick with it.

More about the socioeconomic \_ it is expensive to eat right. I know people at the bottom and their shopping list will be made up of the cheapest foods like bread, pasta and beans etc. They will more often be overweight and will tend to buy low fat food thinking that is the right thing for them. FFS soft drinks are cheaper than good water or milk these days.

So long as telecomms has their wicked way the best investments will be in health providers and big pharma.

\[/steps off soap box\]

Hats off to Michelle for trying to make a difference

### caroline — 2017-08-10T09:16:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-listen-to-this-interview.19986/post-221319>

Da-mo ...... so well said - thanks.

Maybe your daughter could meet up with Michelle one day - and lend her a hand?

This would be an awesome adventure for Matty some day......

Oz loves backpackers!

### Da-mo — 2017-08-10T09:23:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-listen-to-this-interview.19986/post-221320>

It is possible Caroline - she is quite the intrepid traveler. She is a horse dentist so can basically support herself wherever there are horses with owners that can pay her.

Great with animals - its the people that get her worried.

Hippotherapy might be a way she could help Michelle. It has helped many others.

# Thread: Circadian Rhythm, Sepsis & the Mitos

### Scompy — 2017-08-10T07:38:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-rhythm-sepsis-the-mitos.19987/post-221309>

"The hierarchical control from the central clock to peripheral clocks extends to other functions including endocrine, metabolic, immune, and mitochondrial responses. Increasing evidence links the disruption of the clock genes expression with multiple diseases and aging." ... "Thus, this review emphasizes the connection between clock genes, innate immunity and mitochondria in health and sepsis, and the role of melatonin to maintain clock homeostasis."

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28785808](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28785808)

### WalterNL — 2017-08-10T15:45:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-rhythm-sepsis-the-mitos.19987/post-221342>

### Jack Kruse — 2017-08-10T16:03:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-rhythm-sepsis-the-mitos.19987/post-221348>

Infectious disease departments always wonder why they get occassional spikes in infections in ICU's. They also wonder why their nurse's use their healthcare more than expected. We see more resistant bacterial infections in the ICU than anyother place in healthcare. The reason is usually when new equipment enters the unit and is not properly grounded or emits massive electric or magnetic fields. If you bring in your testing gear when a spike in infection shows up it is pretty easy to find. No one realizes that excess nnEMF in the air around us acidifies water everywhere and that includes our cells. acidification lowers pH and helps infections to set up in cells because acidification of cells raises mitochondrial heteroplasmy rates. This is a great example of research done without TeleCom Industry funding showing us how nnEMF lower pH and can lead to infectious disease or to autoimmune conditions. [https://www.laboratoryequipment.com...=6045870&et\_rid=%%subscriberid%%&location=top](https://www.laboratoryequipment.com/news/2017/08/electrical-grounding-technique-may-improve-health-outcomes-nicu-babies?et_cid=6045870&et_rid=45590457&location=top&linkid=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.laboratoryequipment.com%2Fnews%2F2017%2F08%2Felectrical-grounding-technique-may-improve-health-outcomes-nicu-babies%3Fet_cid%3D6045870%26et_rid%3D%25%25subscriberid%25%25%26location%3Dtop)

### Jack Kruse — 2017-08-10T16:05:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-rhythm-sepsis-the-mitos.19987/post-221350>

Moreover, I think we can use the water in our blood to make this diagnosis. Aquaphotomics is the first step to understanding how the battery of life is built and how we can use it to find disease before it becomes deadly. [https://www.patreon.com/posts/13737092](https://www.patreon.com/posts/13737092)

### Scompy — 2017-08-11T04:17:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-rhythm-sepsis-the-mitos.19987/post-221398>

This was just a huge WOW to me...

"...glutamate-induced biophotonic emission does not necessarily correlate with the change in aerobic metabolism. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) can be produced by neural mitochondria as well as by the NADPH oxidase (4, 5). NADPH oxidase can produce ROS whether mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase is completely or incompletely blocked. Glutamate-induced ROS generation in neuronal presynaptic terminals is caused by the activation of the NADPH oxidase and nitric oxide synthases (6).

NMDA receptor (NMDAR) activation by the glutamate increases NADPH oxidase activity, which is the key source of superoxide formation (O2·−) (4). We conclude that the glutamate-induced NMDAR activation and NADPH oxidase activity can lead to overproduction of ROS that causes an increase of biophoton in the brain (7), which seems at odds with the reasoning behind the claims made by Wang et al. (2)."

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5035900/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5035900/)

[https://www.laboratoryequipment.com/news/2017/08/electrical-grounding-technique-may-improve-health-outcomes-nicu-babies?et\_cid=6045870&et\_rid=45590457&location=top&linkid=https://www.laboratoryequipment.com/news/2017/08/electrical-grounding-technique-may-improve-health-outcomes-nicu-babies?et\_cid=6045870&et\_rid=%%subscriberid%%&location=top](https://www.laboratoryequipment.com/news/2017/08/electrical-grounding-technique-may-improve-health-outcomes-nicu-babies?et_cid=6045870&et_rid=45590457&location=top&linkid=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.laboratoryequipment.com%2Fnews%2F2017%2F08%2Felectrical-grounding-technique-may-improve-health-outcomes-nicu-babies%3Fet_cid%3D6045870%26et_rid%3D%25%25subscriberid%25%25%26location%3Dtop)

# Thread: Biohacking Diastasis Recti-visceral vs subcutaneous fat formation & CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-diastasis-recti-visceral-vs-subcutaneous-fat-formation-ct.19991/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ValerieBee — 2017-08-10T22:29:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-diastasis-recti-visceral-vs-subcutaneous-fat-formation-ct.19991/post-221380>

Trying to understand why the body stores fat as visceral rather than subcutaneous. Is it a direct correlation with leptin resistance and inflammatory response? Jack mentions in various places that visceral fat is the most dangerous because it is the most inflammatory. Is it a self-perpetuating cycle where leptin resistance creates an inflammatory environment because the calories not used by the liver cannot be properly used, thus going back to the liver and being "placed into fat storage or stuck inside the liver cell because of high insulin levels." More fat, higher leptin.

I see that CT reduces visceral fat first--is this because it reduces IL-6? And I see some [debate around the internet that CT increases subcutaneous fat, attributed to a Jack blog](https://www.paleohacks.com/dr-jack-kruse/does-the-quilt-s-cold-therapy-protocol-increase-subcutaneous-fat-13788) and some unnamed studies, but I cannot find that blog? (I'd ask the poster on that site, but it is from 2012).

> He says on his blog that it does increase subcutaneous fat. And there are enough credible studies that also point toward an increase in subcutaneous fat. So I think it's probably safe to say, that it increases subcutaneous fat. I think it's also safe to say, based on credible studies, that it also increases brown fat, and increased brown fat activity has an adverse effect on white visceral fat... which is a good thing.

I'm looking to biohack my diastasis recti as part of my slim down and optimal lifestyle, and while I see people have results reducing visceral fat with CT, I really like to understand the why, and look toward prevention rather than just get some flashy results.

I'll start with face dunks, but I'm also inclined to ice pack my tummy directly... how does location on the body affect CT, if you are not simply doing total immersion?

### ValerieBee — 2017-08-10T22:43:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-diastasis-recti-visceral-vs-subcutaneous-fat-formation-ct.19991/post-221382>

@[caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) Wondering if this should be moved to the CT forum?

### Da-mo — 2017-08-11T00:23:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-diastasis-recti-visceral-vs-subcutaneous-fat-formation-ct.19991/post-221388>

I know your questions are about CT effect but I think that you cant divorce the effects of CT on visceral/subcutaneous fat from the O6:O3 effects. They seem to go hand in hand.

If I remember correctly O6/O3 ratio has some effects regarding visceral versus subcutaneous fat.

And the CT protocol makes mention of cold urticaria indicating high tissue O6 and to first load up on O3 before CTing. Brain Freeze is apparently another sign of too high an O6:O3 ratio. JK even put this in red text in the CT easy start guide.

> **\*Note: If you develop cold urticaria at this time****, this is a sign you have high levels of tissue and serum omega six content. Stop the experiment and adjust your diet until you have a blood omega six to three ratio that is below 10 to 1 using a ketogenic paleolithic diet.**

From down in the comments [https://www.jackkruse.com/is-fish-oil-good-or-bad/](https://www.jackkruse.com/is-fish-oil-good-or-bad/)

> _Jack_ says:
> [March 25, 2012 at 10:45 am](https://www.jackkruse.com/is-fish-oil-good-or-bad/#comment-34759)
> If youre at 2:1 and Cting I think your fine…..but you only need to be 4:1 2:1 is where marine mammals who live deep in the cold approach the 1:1 ratio…….when you live in deep cold your ratios then begin to become 03/6 extreme the other way……and this changes adipocyte biology as we understand it……..this is why water based mammals have blubber and land based ones only get it when it is warm………and shred it in cold land based situations……..its all evolution’s work fitting us into the environment and the cell membrane epigenetics is what controls the show.

From Brain Gut 12 also down in the comments [https://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/](https://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/)

> _Ben G_ says:
> [September 16, 2012 at 2:13 am](https://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/#comment-42878)
> Dr. Kruse,
> B/C sumo wrestlers eat a lot of sushi/seafood, they seem to have low visceral fat (and relatively healthier metabolic profile)? Is the Iodine + DHA the reason for it?
> What’s your position on eating kelp for iodine as well?
> [Reply](https://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/#comment-42878)
>
> -   Jack says:
>     [September 16, 2012 at 6:23 am](https://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/#comment-42880)
>     @[BenG](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1088/) This has been mentioned in the literature but I have no idea if it is true. My position on kelp is made pretty clear in Brain Gut 6.

and this research . . . . [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257626/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257626/) Long-Chain Omega-3 Polyunsaturated Fatty Acids May Be Beneficial for Reducing Obesity—A Review

> The preferential reduction in the accumulation of visceral fat has been confirmed in two subsequent studies \[[9](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257626/#B9-nutrients-02-01212),[13](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257626/#B13-nutrients-02-01212)\] and may be mediated primarily by DHA

### caroline — 2017-08-11T02:11:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-diastasis-recti-visceral-vs-subcutaneous-fat-formation-ct.19991/post-221394>

Good thread Valerie ........ You ask great questions!

# Thread: Overstimulation from Sperti Fiji
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overstimulation-from-sperti-fiji.19992/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### karenr — 2017-08-10T23:44:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overstimulation-from-sperti-fiji.19992/post-221387>

So I've been using the Sperti Fiji (UVa and UVB) for about 5 days now, starting very slowly just a few minutes a day, and feeling very overstimulated. Normally I would just chalk that up to my dysautonomia and chronic sleep issues, and I'm at the very beginning of reconfiguring my system. When I was a kid, after a day at the beach I would feel all hyper too.

But a friend who's in good health tried my Sperti lamp for just 1.5 minutes, and said he couldn't sleep that night. Does this have to do with deficiency of early morning light to make melatonin? Does it make sense that UV exposure even at solar noon could be unbalancing without enough balanced red/blue beforehand?

At 43 latitude, we do what we can! I have the Solar Glo lizard light on order.

# Thread: Greetings from Greece
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-greece.19993/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 56

### JohnPxl — 2017-08-11T01:22:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-greece.19993/post-221390>

Hi everyone, my name is John, and I am excited to join this forum.

I am a 35 year old web designer living and working in Athens / Greece with my supportive wife and 2.5 year old twins. English in not my native language, so please bear with me.

My decision to move towards an optimised and healthy lifestyle is quite recent, but I am willing to do whatever it takes to re-configure myself, and live a healthy life for me and my loved ones.

I discovered Dr. Kruse and his website very recently, but his views resonate within me in an un-paralleled manner. I do not want to write a long introduction as my first post here, but would like to mention some key points about my life-journey and my challenges so far.

**1\. I used to hate water.**
In all my life, I hardly remember myself to drink any water. I only used to drink beverages in form of sodas, coke and practically anything except from clean water. Everything begun 4 months ago, when I decided to put myself in a "shock mode" and started to drink 3-4 litres per day. I have kept this until today, and quit all other beverages.

**2\. Kidney stones**
I have suffered a lot from kidney stones. It first happened about 6 years ago, and since then I used to have at least 2-3 incidents per year. Lots of pain, and a lot of stress for my family. I stubbornly continued not to drink any water. Last summer I had a lithotripsy, and did not have any incident since then.

**3\. Scoliosis**
I have a quite bad scoliosis, started at the age of 15 and progressed. It is not that bad to require a surgery, but it is still quite bad. Actually scoliosis is the reason I discovered Dr. Kruse's website, after googling "biohacking scoliosis", which landed me to [this article](https://www.jackkruse.com/biohacking-time-with-methylene-blue/).

**4\. Azoospermia**
Four years ago, while my wife and I were trying to have a baby, I was diagnosed with azoospermia. This is when there is no sperm in the semen. Long story short, after a biopsy at my testicles, there was eventually sperm in there, and today I have two beautiful twins thanks to IVF. When I was young (15) I had a hydroceles surgery at both my testicles, and the doctors said that this might be the cause for my azoospermia. Who knows.

**5\. I have never managed to gain weight**
No matter how much I eat, I have always been a very thin man. I am 50Kg (approx 110 pounds) and about 1.70 m tall (5.5 feet). I don;t know if this is good or bad actually, but I feel I want to gain some mass. Weight is bad for my scoliosis so i just want to get fit and gain some muscle to support my back.

**6\. Heroin Addiction**
When I was younger, I was a heroin addict for four years, but this was 12 years ago. Not a chance I am going back there, but I constantly remind myself that everything in life is a matter of choice, and everything is possible.

**7\. Work and light**
As I said I am a web designer, and for the last ten years I have been mostly living inside, spending about 8-10 hours seated, in front of the computer. Sucks, I know.

**8\. Nutrition**
Poor nutrition for the majority of my life, I used to love junk food. Combine this with smoking and lack of fresh water, and it's definitely a recipe for success and longevity, no? . BUT, during these last four months I completely quit junk food and only cook and eat at home, the best quality food I can find, following a high fat diet. I just ordered Dr. Kruse's Epi-Paleo diet book and waiting for it to arrive by next week, so I can dive into it and see if it works with me.

\----------
**Current status**

After a period of depression, feeling like crap, and complete lack of focus on my work, I felt I had touched the bottom. Then four months ago, during a road trip I did alone, I decided that if I don't take the decision to help myself, no one will. And it all started with water. I can still remember the positive shock my body felt when I poured into it 1 gallon of water in one day. I felt like my batteries turned on.

Now, four months into a more conscious living, I feel much better, but there is a lot of work to do. I am willing to do what it takes, and re-gain myself and my true nature. My short-term goals are:

-   **Reduce work to 4-5 hours daily.** It's possible. I am a freelancer.
-   **Increase sun exposure** as much as possible
-   **Optimise my nutrition**
-   **Reverse my scoliosis** (to the extend it's possible)
-   **Quit Smoking**
-   **Get fit, and workout**

Next week I am going to do some blood tests, to see how my vitamins are. I supplement on magnesium, Krill Oil, Vitamin C and Vitamin D3. I take less that the suggested daily doses, as I feel that my body mass does not need these quantities.

I am sorry for the long post. I will create separate threads for most of my issues/challenges, but wanted to give you the whole picture of where I've been and where I want to go.

I am touched by the supportive vibration and liveliness of this community, and I hope to become an active part of it. I wish you all the best from the depths of my quantum heart.

Cheers
John

### JanSz — 2024-01-04T21:45:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-greece.19993/post-329827>

**Y-Chromosome Infertility (azoospermia) omega-3 omega-6 fatty acids**

Omega-3 and Omega-6 fatty acids **play a significant role in male fertility,** particularly in relation to sperm health.
[A study found that an imbalance between omega-6 and omega-3 fatty acids in sperm has been linked with lipid peroxidation and DNA damage in sperm, indicating a possible correlation to fertility potential](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43032-023-01313-w)[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43032-023-01313-w).
[The study involved 56 infertile men (aged 25–45), and assessed the relationship between the omega-6 to omega-3 fatty acid ratio in sperm and seminal plasma with sperm DNA fragmentation](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43032-023-01313-w)[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43032-023-01313-w).
[Results showed that men with high DNA fragmentation exhibited higher percentages of total saturated, monounsaturated, and omega-6 to omega-3 fatty acid ratios in both sperm and seminal plasma compared to men with low DNA fragmentation](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43032-023-01313-w)[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43032-023-01313-w).

[Moreover, Pearson’s correlation revealed positive associations between the omega-6 to omega-3 fatty acid ratio with sperm lipid peroxidation, DNA fragmentation, and residual histones in both sperm and seminal plasma](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43032-023-01313-w)[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43032-023-01313-w).

[**Overall, these observations suggest that consumption of omega-3 fatty acids may be related to male fertility potential, as it appears that individuals with a high percentage of omega-3 fatty acids have better sperm quality compared to men with a lower omega-3 fatty acid**](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43032-023-01313-w)**[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43032-023-01313-w).**

[However, the role of omega-3 fatty acids in female fertility still remains unclear as the relationship between omega-3 fatty acids and successful IVF treatment in women has shown conflicting results](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/36/Supplement_1/deab130.584/6343802)[2](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/36/Supplement_1/deab130.584/6343802).
[Further studies, including randomized controlled trials of high quality with larger population sizes, are needed in order to thoroughly investigate and conclude any possible association of the beneficial effect of omega–3 fatty acids on female fertility in regard to IVF treatment](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/36/Supplement_1/deab130.584/6343802)[2](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/36/Supplement_1/deab130.584/6343802).

\==========================
I check my fatty acids (including Omega-3) using:

**Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum

///**

### JanSz — 2024-01-04T21:45:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-greece.19993/post-329828>

[The Y chromosome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome)
[is one of two sex chromosomes in therian mammals and other organisms, including humans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome)[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome).
[Along with the X chromosome, it is part of the XY sex-determination system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome)[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome).
[**The presence or absence of the Y chromosome** determines the male or female sex of offspring produced in sexual reproduction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome)[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome).

[In mammals, the Y chromosome contains the SRY gene, which triggers the development of male gonads](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome)[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome).
[The Y chromosome is passed only from male parents to male offspring](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome)[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome).

[The Y chromosome was identified as a sex-determining chromosome by Nettie Stevens at Bryn Mawr College in 1905 during a study of the mealworm Tenebrio molitor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome)[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome).
[The chromosome was given the name “Y” simply to follow on from Henking’s “X” alphabetically](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome)[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_chromosome).

[The Y chromosome spans more than 59 million building blocks of DNA (base pairs) and represents almost 2 percent of the total DNA in cells](https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/chromosome/y/)[2](https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/chromosome/y/).
[It contains many genes involved in sperm production and fertility](https://www.sciencenews.org/article/new-dna-telomere-chromosomes-pangenome)[3](https://www.sciencenews.org/article/new-dna-telomere-chromosomes-pangenome).
**Some males lose the Y chromosome from some of their cells.
[The loss puts them at risk for cancer, Alzheimer’s disease, and other illnesses](https://www.sciencenews.org/article/new-dna-telomere-chromosomes-pangenome)[3](https://www.sciencenews.org/article/new-dna-telomere-chromosomes-pangenome).**

[**It’s important to note that the Y chromosome can vary in length from person to person by millions of DNA bases, meaning that in some people, the chromosome has extra copies of some genes or other bits of DNA**](https://www.sciencenews.org/article/new-dna-telomere-chromosomes-pangenome)[3](https://www.sciencenews.org/article/new-dna-telomere-chromosomes-pangenome).

### JanSz — 2024-01-04T22:04:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-greece.19993/post-329829>

**The relationship between specific omega-3 fatty acids and Y-Chromosome Infertility (azoospermia) is not well-established in the scientific literature.**
Most studies focus on the **overall balance of omega-3 to omega-6 fatty acids** and its impact on general sperm health and male fertility1.

Y-Chromosome Infertility is often caused by deletions of genes in the AZF regions of the Y chromosome1.
These deletions remove several genes, or in rare cases, a single gene.
Loss of this genetic material likely prevents the production of one or more proteins needed for normal sperm cell development1.

While omega-3 fatty acids are known to play a significant role in sperm health and male fertility, it’s important to **note that they are not a cure for genetic conditions like Y-Chromosome Infertility.**

\====================
Additionally, in regard to the
**overall balance of omega-3 to omega-6 fatty acids**
Note that ALL meats that are farmed, cows, goats, sheep's, pigs, birds, fish, usual and organic, all raised by farmers or industrial CAFO, (Concentrated Animal Feeding Operation)
are heavily overloaded with seed oils and what reminds from seeds after oils is extracted.
That is really about (LA) Linoleic Acid.
We are eating 100x some say 1000x more LA than our great grandfathers and mothers.

Just get that:

**Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum**

And post it here so we can see if that is where your problem maybe.

//////

> While omega-3 fatty acids are known to play a significant role in sperm health and male fertility, it’s important to **note that they are not a cure for genetic conditions like Y-Chromosome Infertility.**
>
> \====================
> Additionally, in regard to the
> **overall balance of omega-3 to omega-6 fatty acids**
> Note that ALL meats that are farmed, cows, goats, sheep's, pigs, birds, fish, usual and organic, all raised by farmers or industrial CAFO, (Concentrated Animal Feeding Operation)
> are heavily overloaded with seed oils and what reminds from seeds after oils is extracted.
> That is really about (LA) Linoleic Acid.
> We are eating 100x some say 1000x more LA than our great grandfathers and mothers.
>

### JanSz — 2024-01-05T16:53:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-greece.19993/post-329860>

Lets learn more about azoospermia.

Y-Chromosome Infertility (azoospermia) what does it mean?
Does it mean that the father is able to conceive only females?

..................…

[Y-Chromosome Infertility is a condition that affects the production of sperm, causing male infertility](https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/y-chromosome-infertility/)[1](https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/y-chromosome-infertility/).
[**This means it can be difficult or impossible for affected men to father children**](https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/y-chromosome-infertility/)[1](https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/y-chromosome-infertility/).
[The condition can manifest in several ways: an affected man’s body may produce no mature sperm cells (azoospermia), fewer than the usual number of sperm cells (oligospermia), or sperm cells that are abnormally shaped or do not move properly](https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/y-chromosome-infertility/)[1](https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/y-chromosome-infertility/).
The Y chromosome has a region called AZF (azoospermia factor).
[The presence of deletions in this chromosomal region is related to problems in spermatogenesis](https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/y-chromosome-infertility/)[2](https://www.invitra.com/en/how-is-male-infertility-caused-by-y-chromosome-microdeletions/).
[For infertile men, microdeletion in the Y chromosome is one of the common genetic factors leading to spermatogenic disorders](https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/y-chromosome-infertility/)[3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10815-020-02031-x).
However, it’s important to note that Y-Chromosome Infertility does not mean that the father is able to conceive only females.
The sex of a baby is determined by whether an X (female) or Y (male) sperm cell from the father fertilizes the mother’s egg.
A man with Y-Chromosome Infertility may have issues with sperm production or function, but this does not directly influence the sex of the offspring.
If a man with this condition is able to conceive, the sex of the baby would still be determined by the usual process of sexual reproduction.

.....

### JanSz — 2024-01-05T16:57:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-greece.19993/post-329861>

**does azoospermia has any correlation with fatty acids or metabolism?

Yes, there is some evidence to suggest a correlation between azoospermia, fatty acids, and metabolism.**
[In terms of fatty acids, a study found that the composition of fatty acids in the spermatozoa plasma membrane is directly related to sperm motility](https://rbej.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12958-021-00792-3)[1](https://rbej.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12958-021-00792-3).
[Another study showed that spermatozoa from astheno-oligozoospermic bulls (which includes conditions like azoospermia) showed alterations in the metabolism of fatty acids](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fvets.2021.755560/full)[2](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fvets.2021.755560/full).
[Moreover, men with increased levels of omega-6s in their blood had higher levels of luteinizing hormone, a characteristic of primary Leydig cell failure](https://omega3innovations.com/blog/want-to-improve-sperm-health-lets-talk-about-diet/)[3](https://omega3innovations.com/blog/want-to-improve-sperm-health-lets-talk-about-diet/).
[As for metabolism, various metabolic pathways, including d-glutamine and d-glutamate metabolism, taurine and hypotaurine metabolism, pyruvate metabolism, the citrate cycle, and alanine, aspartate and glutamate metabolism, were found to be disrupted in patients with non-obstructive azoospermia](https://www.mdpi.com/1422-0067/18/2/238)[4](https://www.mdpi.com/1422-0067/18/2/238).
[Another study suggested that abnormal energy metabolism and oxidative stress induced by mitochondrial dysfunction may be decisive factors involved in spermatogenic disorders](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fgene.2023.1115669/full)[5](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fgene.2023.1115669/full).
However, while these studies suggest a correlation, more research is needed to fully understand the complex relationships between azoospermia, fatty acids, and metabolism.
It’s also important to note that these factors are just a part of a larger picture, **and azoospermia can be influenced by a variety of genetic, environmental, and lifestyle factors.**

### Cameron Horner — 2024-01-07T02:15:03+0200

Yes you are right i have the diagnosis of azoospermia.

Azoospermia is either obstruction or non obstruction. For me it is non obstruction.

The reason for my azoospermia is genetic; y chromosome does not contain the reproductive genes.

It’s possible science may not have found the exact pathway that the mtFAS uses to repair the Y chromosome

John’s azoospermia may be similar to mine; karyotype - John had twins through IVF - they would have extracted the sperm directly from his testicles - it would not have been natural insemination

It is possible i have sperm in my testicles depending on which AZF region is remaining on my Y chromosome. But because there is so little production due to a deformed Y chromosome i would need the same process. Urologist would advise.

Omega 6s etc and other lifestyle factors DO impact sperm production. The mtfas definitely has something to do with production. But can it repair Y chromosome AZF regions. That is the question that most likely we do not know at this time.

I was thinking the damage may have occurred during conception - which is also possible.

But with the damage done I don’t think there is an undo button to retrieve the genes - unless the mitochondria have a memory

I was hoping telomeres might hold a clue but i don't think they contain genes.

but it seems chromosomes when damaged access their partner to get the instructions for repair. Sadly men generally only have one Y chromosome. Sometimes two (XYY) yet even they have fertility issues.

[https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(99)00311-6/fulltext](https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736\(99\)00311-6/fulltext)

Life is fascinating and yet sad.

### JanSz — 2024-01-09T03:41:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-greece.19993/post-330005>

[The Methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase **(MTHFR)** gene plays a significant role in male infertility, particularly in conditions like **azoospermia**](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/24/9/2361/600714)[1](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/24/9/2361/600714)[2](https://methyl-life.com/blogs/mthfr/mthfr-infertility)[3](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0121147).

[MTHFR is a key regulatory enzyme involved in folate metabolism, DNA synthesis, and remethylation reactions](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/24/9/2361/600714)[1](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/24/9/2361/600714).
[Mutations in the MTHFR gene, such as the MTHFR C677T mutation, have been identified as risk factors for azoospermia, a condition characterized by the absence of sperm in the ejaculate](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/24/9/2361/600714)[2](https://methyl-life.com/blogs/mthfr/mthfr-infertility).

[In addition to genetic mutations, epigenetic mechanisms like promoter hypermethylation could also play a role in azoospermia](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/24/9/2361/600714)[1](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/24/9/2361/600714).
[A study found that the MTHFR promoter region was hypermethylated in testicular biopsies of patients with non-obstructive azoospermia](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/24/9/2361/600714)[1](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/24/9/2361/600714).
[This hypermethylation in testis DNA from non-obstructive azoospermia patients is specific and not due to a general methylation defect, suggesting that **epigenetic silencing of MTHFR could play a role in azoospermic infertility**](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/24/9/2361/600714)[1](https://academic.oup.com/humrep/article/24/9/2361/600714).

[However, the exact mechanisms of how MTHFR affects azoospermia are still not fully understood and further research is needed](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0121147)[3](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0121147).
\===================
\===================
[@Cameron Horner](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21407/)

If you want to experiment, with the thoughts about your azoospermia, get:

homocysteine test

If your homocysteine comes about 5-8 (µmol/L)
forget about it.
If it comes much higher, you may want to think about lowering it.
Lover values, other than making your metabolism healthier,
may (hopefully) change azoospermia.

Just a guess. The test cost $69 or $49 in my neighborhood.

### [Homocysteine Blood Test | Walk-In Lab](https://www.walkinlab.com/products/view/homocysteine-blood-test-plasma)

Quick Facts Sample: Blood (plasma or serum) Fasting: Not required Turnaround Time: 1–2 business days. May take longer based on weather, holiday, or la

 www.walkinlab.com

/////////

# Thread: Penmanship
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Stardust — 2017-08-11T07:52:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221403>

My 7 year old who was diagnosed with ADHD has troubled penmanship. She is a very good speller and can form sentences just fine. Getting is on paper is a struggle. It is hard to motivate her to write. I saw no improvement with occupational therapy. Does anyone have any recommendations?

### caroline — 2017-08-11T11:53:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221415>

maybe this is way off base - my daughter has dyslexia.

It seems that it takes many different forms. When my daughter reads - it is all just separate words to her with no connection.
some people see colours, for others the words just fall off the page.

A friend of ours has a son who had difficulty reading - so they gave him comic books. It made a huge difference when this boy could read something that was of interest. He is a really bright kid btw.

I understand that your daughter's problem is writing - but something is not connecting properly I would think

What is her diet like? Is she playing outside a lot?

### Sue-UK — 2017-08-11T13:25:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221423>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=221403)
>
> My 7 year old who was diagnosed with ADHD has troubled penmanship. She is a very good speller and can form sentences just fine. Getting is on paper is a struggle. It is hard to motivate her to write. I saw no improvement with occupational therapy. Does anyone have any recommendations?

I taught my children that writing could be fun by getting them to write about what interested them ... And if they couldn't spell a word or just wanted to be creative (DD loves art), they could incorporate pictures or find a different way of expressing a word ... So a line of writing might include a picture of a spaceship, or a dinosaur or a princess .... If they were writing the word red, they could write the word itself in red ... (DS2 learned how to spell the colours from that.) My DD was a bit older so she would write the word circle as a circle, the words "upside down"....upside down  it became a game to find words and phrases that she could express in a different way... It was a way of giving them self confidence - if writing can be seen to be interesting, and fun, it became "easy" (and I think it helps form neural networks between different parts of the brain), it honours what they know rather than focusing on what they can't do, and it helped motivation.

### Stardust — 2017-08-11T22:42:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221478>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=221415)
>
> maybe this is way off base - my daughter has dyslexia.
>
> It seems that it takes many different forms. When my daughter reads - it is all just separate words to her with no connection.
> some people see colours, for others the words just fall off the page.
>
> A friend of ours has a son who had difficulty reading - so they gave him comic books. It made a huge difference when this boy could read something that was of interest. He is a really bright kid btw.
>
> I understand that your daughter's problem is writing - but something is not connecting properly I would think
>
> What is her diet like? Is she playing outside a lot?

In the beginning, we thought it was Dyslexia. Well, the school thought it was dyslexia. We had her assessed and they determined she was not. I agree with the not connecting properly. Her motor skills in general are lacking. Her diet is 75% Epi-Paleo. My kids have had a more difficult time shifting over than I did. She does play outside most of the day. She actually dreads being indoors which is great. I'm going to sign her up for Piano class and see what other motor skill activities I can add. How old is your daughter? I was diagnosed with dyslexia as a child as well.

### Stardust — 2017-08-11T22:46:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221479>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=221423)
>
> I taught my children that writing could be fun by getting them to write about what interested them ... And if they couldn't spell a word or just wanted to be creative (DD loves art), they could incorporate pictures or find a different way of expressing a word ... So a line of writing might include a picture of a spaceship, or a dinosaur or a princess .... If they were writing the word red, they could write the word itself in red ... (DS2 learned how to spell the colours from that.) My DD was a bit older so she would write the word circle as a circle, the words "upside down"....upside down  it became a game to find words and phrases that she could express in a different way... It was a way of giving them self confidence - if writing can be seen to be interesting, and fun, it became "easy" (and I think it helps form neural networks between different parts of the brain), it honours what they know rather than focusing on what they can't do, and it helped motivation.

Those are some great strategies! I need to keep her interested in school. She is so bright! I do not want her to begin to dislike learning because writing is not fun for her. She lacks self-confidence in her writing ability. She is in 3rd grade this year and by next year she will have to be writing so much more.

### caroline — 2017-08-12T02:46:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221488>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=221478)
>
> In the beginning, we thought it was Dyslexia. Well, the school thought it was dyslexia. We had her assessed and they determined she was not. I agree with the not connecting properly. Her motor skills in general are lacking. Her diet is 75% Epi-Paleo. My kids have had a more difficult time shifting over than I did. She does play outside most of the day. She actually dreads being indoors which is great. I'm going to sign her up for Piano class and see what other motor skill activities I can add. How old is your daughter? I was diagnosed with dyslexia as a child as well.

I am a grandmother .....my daughter loves cooking so she can read cookbooks and really connects with that.

Actually - do you cook with your daughter? maybe reading a recipe and writing out in simple ingredient points would interest her?

My younger daughter was a very difficult eater. We got her to chose a recipe and then help shop for stuff and then help make it.

That made a huge difference - she then had control over the process.

### Sue-UK — 2017-08-12T15:14:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221511>

Its been a bumpy road since DS2 was a young child, light sensitive, food phobic, water phobic, anxious, delayed speech development ... (turned out he is incredibly sensitive to the effects of fluoride). Watching his inner doctor guide him to recovery when it got the chance is one of my greatest joys. A lot of moments of grace. We chose the guitar over the piano ... DS2 was keen to learn electric guitar, the lessons were geared mainly around acoustic but with enough electric to keep him motivated. Its turned out that he is good at it, but its not his passion, even the electric guitar. For lessons, he would do just enough practice, but if he'd done more because he was passionate about it, he would be exceptional. He sometimes goes outside and sits under the trees with his acoustic guitar, and every time he does I have another moment of grace. .

### caroline — 2017-08-13T03:35:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221572>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=221479)
>
> Those are some great strategies! I need to keep her interested in school. She is so bright! I do not want her to begin to dislike learning because writing is not fun for her. She lacks self-confidence in her writing ability. She is in 3rd grade this year and by next year she will have to be writing so much more.

What is your daughter most interested in? I expect that would propel her forward ....as Sue just said.

Another thought .....my youngest daughter always got really, really bad reports about her hand writing. She would always come home crying because her teaches said she wasn't trying hard enough. The poor little thing would just be sobbing and saying "Mommy - I am trying so hard"

It turns out a Doctor said she may have arthritis in her fingers ....This is so many years later so when I think about it seems a little odd but obviously her fingers were stiff for some reason.

She now has children of her own but her handwriting is still quite large and almost child like. Btw - she is a very bright young woman and her daughter has been identified as gifted.......so it is nothing to do with intelligence. She has a science degree ....and maybe more telling .....she is an incredible people person.

> It turns out a Doctor said she may have arthritis in her fingers ....This is so many years later so when I think about it seems a little odd but obviously her fingers were stiff for some reason.

Btw - she is a very bright young woman and her daughter has been identified as gifted.......so it is nothing to do with intelligence. She has a science degree ....and maybe more telling .....she is an incredible people person.

I cook with her every so often. She loves SCIENCE! Last year it was apparent to her that her handwriting was dramatically different then her classmates. It weighed on her pretty bad. It was also mentioned at every intervention meeting we had with the school. She is very bright as well and often says she is trying her best .

### Stardust — 2017-08-15T21:02:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221707>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=221511)

> Its been a bumpy road since DS2 was a young child, light sensitive, food phobic, water phobic, anxious, delayed speech development ... (turned out he is incredibly sensitive to the effects of fluoride). Watching his inner doctor guide him to recovery when it got the chance is one of my greatest joys. A lot of moments of grace. We chose the guitar over the piano ... DS2 was keen to learn electric guitar, the lessons were geared mainly around acoustic but with enough electric to keep him motivated. Its turned out that he is good at it, but its not his passion, even the electric guitar. For lessons, he would do just enough practice, but if he'd done more because he was passionate about it, he would be exceptional. He sometimes goes outside and sits under the trees with his acoustic guitar, and every time he does I have another moment of grace. .

Isn't is amazing to watch recovering right in front of you?! My daughter has come a long way as well. Passion can lead to amazing things. I suspect she will like piano but it might not be her passion either lol. She loves sewing, science, math and roaming my parents 5 acres. Can't say I blame her.

### caroline — 2017-08-16T06:16:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221750>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=221706)

When it comes right down it it ....does it really matter what her handwriting looks like? Obviously she has many skills and interests.

Think about it .....you said she loves sewing- so her motor skills are good enuf for that? It seems odd that her hand writing is less than ideal?

Maybe something happened earlier on at her school and she was made to feel very self conscious about her writing????

Maybe her teachers should lay off - and applaud the things she is great at?!!

What instrument would she prefer? Have you thought of a ukulele? they are pretty easy to play when just starting and may give her a sense of accomplishment.......

### caroline — 2017-08-16T06:19:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221751>

I bet Jack would have some interesting insight into this......

### Sue-UK — 2017-08-16T12:30:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221756>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=221707)
>
> Isn't is amazing to watch recovering right in front of you?! My daughter has come a long way as well. Passion can lead to amazing things. I suspect she will like piano but it might not be her passion either lol. She loves sewing, science, math and roaming my parents 5 acres. Can't say I blame her.

I did this with travel for 3 years when the kids were going to miss school (which at 6 and 4 wasn't such an issue with the school, but it helped the parent/teacher relationship that we weren't ignoring their education while we were away). But we went shopping and they each picked a book to use as a holiday journal, so their choice of colour and pattern/pictures on the cover were their own. Each day they would write a few lines on what in that day had interested them. It became their holiday souvenir. If they were enjoying something and wanted to put it in their journal later they could choose a postcard, or they would draw a picture, stick in a bus ticket etc.... The books they had chosen, a pen, colouring stuff and a glue stick ...their own interests and imagination, and no pressure. But the more they were interested in something the more they would write .... Maybe your daughter could journal what she notices on her roams in your parents 5 acres? And/or one on other subjects she finds interesting, perhaps a journal for each? The trick is to let them write spontaneously, no worrying about their handwriting or spelling, only help if asked, and if there are days when they really don't feel like doing it, or don't want to write more than a line, don't worry about it.

### Stardust — 2017-08-20T20:40:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221969>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=221750)
>
> When it comes right down it it ....does it really matter what her handwriting looks like? Obviously she has many skills and interests.
>
> Think about it .....you said she loves sewing- so her motor skills are good enuf for that? It seems odd that her hand writing is less than ideal?
>
> Maybe something happened earlier on at her school and she was made to feel very self conscious about her writing????
>
> Maybe her teachers should lay off - and applaud the things she is great at?!!
>
> What instrument would she prefer? Have you thought of a ukulele? they are pretty easy to play when just starting and may give her a sense of accomplishment.......

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=221751)
>
> One other thought ......bread making is quite therapeutic. All the kneading may give you some insight into how agile her hands/fingers are?
>
> What could be more rewarding than baking a loaf of bread!
>
> The more I think about it ....why does it matter so much about her handwriting. Maybe she should become a Doctor!
>
> I bet Jack would have some interesting insight into this......

She absolutely has many skills and interests. She actually is always working with slime, putty and playdough. Writing increases in 3rd grade so this will be an interesting year. I don't have to worry about teachers this year because I'm the teacher. I pulled her out to homeschool. We saw regression when we vaccinated her at 2.5 years old and California's SB277 forced vaccine law was a problem. She left the school system with insecurity about writing though. I want to build that confidence back up. Piano is her preferred instrument I never considered the ukulele. I will look into that. Thank you Caroline!

### Stardust — 2017-08-20T20:48:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221971>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=221756)
>
> I did this with travel for 3 years when the kids were going to miss school (which at 6 and 4 wasn't such an issue with the school, but it helped the parent/teacher relationship that we weren't ignoring their education while we were away). But we went shopping and they each picked a book to use as a holiday journal, so their choice of colour and pattern/pictures on the cover were their own. Each day they would write a few lines on what in that day had interested them. It became their holiday souvenir. If they were enjoying something and wanted to put it in their journal later they could choose a postcard, or they would draw a picture, stick in a bus ticket etc.... The books they had chosen, a pen, colouring stuff and a glue stick ...their own interests and imagination, and no pressure. But the more they were interested in something the more they would write .... Maybe your daughter could journal what she notices on her roams in your parents 5 acres? And/or one on other subjects she finds interesting, perhaps a journal for each? The trick is to let them write spontaneously, no worrying about their handwriting or spelling, only help if asked, and if there are days when they really don't feel like doing it, or don't want to write more than a line, don't worry about it.

How awesome!! What a fun thing to do while traveling. We started a journal with an idea from a homeschooling workshop I went to. We basically write messages back and forth. She has loved it so far! I love the idea of journaling at my parents. She climbs trees, makes "survival" tools, talks to bugs, builds shelters and many more things out there. Running out of ideas to write about will not be possible. Sue, thank you for all the suggestions!

### Penny — 2017-08-20T21:20:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221975>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=221971)
>
> How awesome!! What a fun thing to do while traveling. We started a journal with an idea from a homeschooling workshop I went to. We basically write messages back and forth. She has loved it so far! I love the idea of journaling at my parents. She climbs trees, makes "survival" tools, talks to bugs, builds shelters and many more things out there. Running out of ideas to write about will not be possible. Sue, thank you for all the suggestions!

I love how Montessori does hand writing - they trace sandpaper letters glued onto a wooden block - then they are constantly pulling pegs from puzzles (3 finger grip) - then they have a moveable alphabet where they make their own words, then read them, then write them - they are writing cursive at the age of 3... it's amazing to see:
[http://www.infomontessori.com/language/handwriting-introduction.htm](http://www.infomontessori.com/language/handwriting-introduction.htm)

### Sue-UK — 2017-08-21T09:58:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221989>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=221971)
>
> How awesome!! What a fun thing to do while traveling. We started a journal with an idea from a homeschooling workshop I went to. We basically write messages back and forth. She has loved it so far! I love the idea of journaling at my parents. She climbs trees, makes "survival" tools, talks to bugs, builds shelters and many more things out there. Running out of ideas to write about will not be possible.

Another thought is a special pen for journaling... I was watching a you tube video on making a slime pen using the inside of a biro through a plastic Walmart bought syringe, and then filling the syringe with coloured and glittery slime. My kids would have loved that ... I can feel a Nana moment coming on ......

Another thought is chalk ... writing in chalk on a driveway, a smooth rock, chalk board signs to go in her shelters .... If a child writes "smooth rock" on a rock, it relates the written pattern of words to the visual pattern of the object. Or that written symbol patterns are a meaningful communication .. This way and arrows ..... (Name)'s den .....

How about a map? - she can make a map of a section of the 5 acres, mark or draw in the trees or things of interest and then write in what it is .... oak tree, lots of x bugs/birds/flowers here .... favourite shelter ....

### EvaJoh — 2017-08-25T10:30:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-222221>

Wait a bit more. Don’t stop the [occupational therapy services](http://www.newmummycompany.ca/services/occupational-therapy/) in the middle of the course. If you haven’t found any improvement under the present program then it shows that you need more intense services not a discontinuation of services.

Make a conversation with your OT regarding your child’s disabilities, behavior, daily routine etc. They can reschedule the therapy programs for your child. It can cause if the OT doesn’t understand your child’s problem properly.

Consult yet another OT. She/he might understand your child’s problem.

>

> [Penny said:](/goto/post?id=222312)

# Thread: New Israeli podcast talking about kids.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ScottishEmma — 2017-08-11T08:49:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/post-221406>

YAY! A new podcast - my favourite!

### ScottishEmma — 2017-08-11T15:54:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/post-221434>

This was a fabulous podcast. My kids now watch no TV and will never play with a tablet - whilst I'm still breathing.

I'm also homeschooling because I'm no way letting my kids sit in a blue lit classroom all day!

I thought the bit at the end was interesting talking about low dopamine people not understanding the science. I fear that they might but that they just don't care enough.

I am that 'crazy' person right now trying to share some of this information but people don't want to know, even if they believe it on some level.

### caroline — 2017-08-12T10:46:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/post-221505>

great podcast - really straightforward and to the point. There is always so much more to learn.

### shiran — 2017-08-12T12:43:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/post-221506>

> [ScottishEmma said:](/goto/post?id=221434)
>
> This was a fabulous podcast. My kids now watch no TV and will never play with a tablet - whilst I'm still breathing.
>
> I'm also homeschooling because I'm no way letting my kids sit in a blue lit classroom all day!
>
> I thought the bit at the end was interesting talking about low dopamine people not understanding the science. I fear that they might but that they just don't care enough.
>
> I am that 'crazy' person right now trying to share some of this information but people don't want to know, even if they believe it on some level.

I am the same "crazy " person as you...
yesterday my closest family said it to me straight in my face... and made fun of me
That was during the family dinner when they saw me for the first time with my blue blockers on .
My response was straight forward...
It's amazing how much ignorance
It amazes me !
That low dopamine in people can Harm sometimes others... only with words
but my light won't let it go through...

### caroline — 2017-08-12T13:21:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/post-221507>

keep moving forward on your optimal journey ........it would be lovely to have the support of your family, but this is all about you right now.

Don't be deterred by anyone.

### Sue-UK — 2017-08-12T14:28:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/post-221509>

I still get the odd bit of light hearted teasing, but it doesn't bother me if what they say has me laughing with them. I might say why I'm doing something if they are interested, but I don't try and justify myself, and I won't take any shit. I would tell someone if they were going to be unpleasant, change the subject. My DD has had conversations with her friends (usually after a glass or three or more....) about who has the craziest parent. Apparently I'm still the winner by a long shot .... I've told her the day I'm not top of the list is the day I know I'm not trying hard enough.

### caroline — 2017-08-13T03:24:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/post-221571>

love that Sue ^^^^!!!

# Thread: The delivery of blogs will change from here on in as of  8/11/2017
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-delivery-of-blogs-will-change-from-here-on-in-as-of-8-11-2017.19996/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2017-08-11T14:51:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-delivery-of-blogs-will-change-from-here-on-in-as-of-8-11-2017.19996/post-221429>

The blogs will no longer be available as they have been. Silver, Gold, and Paltinum members will get the content as part of their membership and Bronze can participate too by signing up for membership. They will get blog access only from this point forward.

I have given 6 years of content to the public for free 100% of the time. That has come to end.

I am no focusing in on my tribe from here on out. SKIN IN THE GAME matters deeply to me.

Members will recieve the new blog today in your member dashboard. The blogs will now be part of your membership package. It will no longer be available as they used to be. Comments will also be limited to members as well. You will get some information on this today from support if you are a member of Jackkruse.com from here on in.

Here is the lead in for the new blog called REALITY #15: ANIMAL PHOTOSYNTHESIS
Released Aug 11 at 1:00am on Patreon.
Here is the link for those of you who are interested but lurkers.
[www.patreon.com/DrJackKruse](http://www.patreon.com/DrJackKruse)
THE BLOG TAKE HOME : Are there truly quantum effects or phenomena intrinsic to a specific thermodynamic process that offer an operational advantage over their classical counterpart? Most people think that sunlight hitting water is the key mechanism to make the battery for life. That view point is myopic. Quantum mechanics allows us to create a battery that can collect and distrobute energy on demand and this is the kind of battery tissues that are alive would need. It represent what we observe in the living state. When we need energy our tissues deliver. How do they do this? After all thermodynamics should be sensitive to the underlying microscopic description. For example, the efficiency of an engine should always be limited by the Carnot bound irrespective of whether the working medium is comprised of quantum or classical components. However, for non- equilibrium protocols the question is more subtle. For instance very recently it has been demonstrated that it is possible to use non-equilibrium short cuts to adiabaticity to boost the power of engine cycles without compromising efficiency (Deng et al. 2013, del Campo et al. 2014). One may then wonder whether it is in the finite time operation of devices that quantum mechanics offers an advantage biology is unaware of. The present blog provides an example of a process where quantum correlations provide an huge advantage to living state.

### Billybats — 2017-08-11T15:37:58+0300

### drezy — 2017-08-12T01:13:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-delivery-of-blogs-will-change-from-here-on-in-as-of-8-11-2017.19996/post-221482>

Doc, I'm in it for the long game and I am very appreciative of how you helped my mom and family.

Any benefit that, thanks to you, I map out on myself gets applied immediately to my only child. In the long game I've got only **one** shot.

As a member do I really want to filter through the 2x per month accusations that I'm some cult member? Not really. It adds comedic value but no real value to my son's benefit.

With @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) and I you've gone and let the Polacks in though. We are tough straight talkers by culture. Based on some of your last pictures you are abusing yourself with your environment and you look like shit, Jack. How's that for not being sycophantic? Get back up in fighting shape, sir.

I know that you know how to reverse all of this so I'm not overly concerned about you.

I need you at your best, and doubly so my son, and all that entails.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-08-12T05:50:24+0300
