Germans are straight talkers, too.You first for a while, Doc!

### b.pezzia — 2017-08-12T15:58:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-delivery-of-blogs-will-change-from-here-on-in-as-of-8-11-2017.19996/post-221514>

Jack,i can say that i was waiting for this couple months now,don't ask me why i just knew it.

I will tell you that i'm following your site and your twitter for 18 months and is the first time in my life that i decided to become a member somewhere, first because of the unique flux of information and second because of your style which tells me a lot about why you do the things that you do.

But if i'm not getting indiscrete i would really like to know as a member of this tribe why the chief decides to change how we walk through this wood.
If you don't want to elaborate in this publicly pls send me a pm.
Anyway as @[Lahelada](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6531/) said,i appreciate all the work that you have done till now and i'm very grateful for every word that you wrote in the blogs,i wish i knew you before the "gracious" death of my diabetic father in the hands of highly regarded but mis/un educated doctors.

Die less...and Live forever as i say..

### Jack Kruse — 2017-08-12T16:33:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-delivery-of-blogs-will-change-from-here-on-in-as-of-8-11-2017.19996/post-221515>

Why am I doing it? Simple.........I want people with skin in the game. For too long I've been diluted by those on the fringe. My tribe is my passion and I deliver value. If you don't think so........then go find this information somewhere else.........Good Luck with that.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-08-12T16:35:47+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2017-08-12T16:37:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-delivery-of-blogs-will-change-from-here-on-in-as-of-8-11-2017.19996/post-221517>

The blogs will be open to all Silver Gold and Platinum members as part of your mebership. Members got the blog yesterday from our web folks.

### b.pezzia — 2017-08-12T17:58:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-delivery-of-blogs-will-change-from-here-on-in-as-of-8-11-2017.19996/post-221523>

Jack,your decision is respected and everyone here will decide what to do from now on,but still... judging by myself when i started reading your blogs i couldn't understand many of it because i don't have the academic background but insisted and now i can understand a glimpse of what you are teaching and even this little has helped me a lot 18months now.

Can you say that i don't have skin in the game because i don't pay for the infos? I disagree because i put very big effort to balance my time and energy studying your blogs your tweets your podcasts,to take notes to compare and reread again with being able to withstand a very stressful job,manage a big family in a social environment that (here in Greece) changes rapidly and very hard.
Do i give value to your work ?
Yes of course i think i explained myself above.
Do i pay for your work?
No,i'm THANKFUL to you because with your work realise things that can protect me and my family and maybe influence my social surrounding.
Why you didn't do this from the beginning?
Didn't you know that you will meet people along the way that will dilute your words and try to " sabotage" your work?
or simply it's the time that you said (of course you have every right) I had enough?
Again sorry if i'm being an ass with too many questions but as i said above it's my first some as a "tribe guy" so

### Jack Kruse — 2017-08-12T18:36:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-delivery-of-blogs-will-change-from-here-on-in-as-of-8-11-2017.19996/post-221527>

The library is like a candy store where everything is free.......if you want those concepts connected to save you time you'll become a member. It is that simple. When people don't value your worth the simple answer is to make them see what life is without your complete contribution. That time has arrived for you. Decision time.

Why being a doctor can be a slippery slope for some humans. When you’re at work, be fully absorbed for your patients. When it’s time to call it a day, completely detach yourself from work and become absorbed in the other areas of your life. This viewpoint is not well received by those who employ physicians and by many physicians themselves. This idea is trained into the physician mindset. It is unwise.
If you don’t detach, you’ll never fully be present or engaged at work or at home. You’ll be under constant strain, even if minimally. Your sleep will suffer. Your relationships will be shallow. Your life will not be happy. You will burnout. The key to a optimal life is to always remember you are worthless to others if you are not good enough for yourself. Wise up.

### caroline — 2017-08-13T03:50:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-delivery-of-blogs-will-change-from-here-on-in-as-of-8-11-2017.19996/post-221573>

This has to be a priority if our health is important to us. Our Ozzie $$$ sucks compared to the U.S. greenback but we are committed to Dr. Kruse and staying healthy ......we have decided that nothing is more important.

The last six years has been an incredible learning curve and I am so lucky and grateful to have found a like minded partner for this optimal journey.

We are sticking like glue.....

# Thread: Leptin and Sleep Apnea
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-and-sleep-apnea.19997/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jason Coates — 2017-08-11T15:22:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-and-sleep-apnea.19997/post-221430>

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28796527?dopt=Abstract](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28796527?dopt=Abstract)

Experimental studies using animal models of a low leptin state such as leptin deficiency have shown that leptin regulates sleep architecture, upper airway patency, ventilatory function, and hypercapnic ventilatory response. However, findings from human studies do not consistently support the data from the animal models. The effect of leptin on the pathophysiology of obstructive sleep apnea is being investigated, but the results of studies have been confounded by its diurnal variation and the short-term effects of feeding, adiposity, age, and gender. Improved study design and methods of assessing functional leptin levels, specifically its central versus peripheral effects, will improve our understanding of the role of leptin in sleep apnea.

# Thread: Hi from UK, England
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-uk-england.19999/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sajid Mahmood — 2017-08-11T21:53:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-uk-england.19999/post-221474>

A big hello to the Kruser Klub! My name is Sajid Mahmood. I am a Pakistani descent. My mother and Father are both Pakistani descent. I live at the 53rd latitude (52.95th to exact) in United Kingdom, England. I am 41 years of age and have 2 kids. Boy aged 5 and girl aged 3. I am a Taxi driver. I'm on a mission to keep myself and my family as healthy as possible. One of my other main goals in life is to develop my son into a successful soccer/football player. I hardly have any advanced background knowledge in science. I have been following Dr Jack Kruse on the internet for about a year now. I bumped onto Jack on one of his YouTube podcasts. The first time I listened to him I hardly understood a thing he was saying. He was so intellectual, sounded very convincing, gave an explanation for everything he was presenting (hardly any of it understood by the way lol). I was so curious and intrigued by his work, I kept following him and listening to his podcasts on YouTube, and also had been reading his Facebook page. Dr Jack Kruse has even had the kind generosity of answering dozens of my questions on Twitter. In time, I was learning and became familiar with the terminology in time. I have just become a silver member recently and was eager to become one long time ago. So now I'm finally here. Before I bumped into Jack, I suffered from winter Asthma, hay fever, acid reflux, indigestion, insomnia. A year and a half ago, my winter Asthma was so bad I ended up with pneumonia. Since following Jack, I seem to be coping much better with my Asthma in winter. Hay fever symptoms seemed to have improved. Sleep problems and insomnia have improved, although I do have a lot of family stress problems. Very rarely get any acid reflux and indigestion.

I would just like to thank Jack for everything I've learned from him since following him. I have learned so much from him, and he has made a massive difference to my life. I would like to give him a special thanks for all the useful and important information he has given to the public for free. I wish you and you're family a happy, long and successful life! We need more people like you on this planet! There will never be another Dr Jack Kruse! You're by miles the absolute best Doctor in the world! Lost of love Jack!! You're a Legend!!! I hope this increases your dopamine levels! lol!

# Thread: Taurine - Light - GABA efflux in the retina... this explains a lot:-)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/taurine-light-gaba-efflux-in-the-retina-this-explains-a-lot.20002/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Penny — 2017-08-12T22:29:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/taurine-light-gaba-efflux-in-the-retina-this-explains-a-lot.20002/post-221556>

**Effects of Taurine and Light on Retinal GABA Content and the Efflux of 14C-GABA and 14C-Aspartate from Frog Retina**

-   Authors
-   [Authors and affiliations](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007%2F978-1-4899-0117-0_51#authorsandaffiliations)

-   Jasmine E. Haroutounian
-   Andranik M. Petrosian

Chapter

-   [4 Citations](http://www.bookmetrix.com/detail/chapter/e74d6558-47d7-4dc4-ab79-261476dc1d91#citations)
-   [143 Downloads](http://www.bookmetrix.com/detail/chapter/e74d6558-47d7-4dc4-ab79-261476dc1d91#downloads)

**Summary**
GABA content of isolated, dark adapted frog retina was found to be 3.15 ± 0.28 mM. After 30 minutes of exposure to intense light (200 1x), retinal GABA levels increased about 70%. Interestingly, incubation of dark adapted retina for 30 minutes with medium containing 0.4 mM taurine also led to a 70% increase in GABA levels. Since the light-induced elevation in GABA content was reduced over 50% by a simultaneous injection of 0.02 mM strychinine, it is likely that the light-induced GABA change is partly mediated by the release of taurine from the retina seen after light exposure. However, incubation of isolated retina with medium containing increasing concentrations of taurine (1, 2 and 20 mM), caused a progressive rise in 14C-GABA efflux from retina that was preloaded with 2.2 μM GABA and exposed to dim light (0.05 1x). It was also shown that taurine (1 and 5 mM) dramatically reduced 14C-aspartate efflux from retina preloaded with radioactive aspartate and exposed to dim light conditions. By comparison, intense light stimulation (40 1x) reduced basal 14C-aspartate efflux while dark exposure increased 14C-aspartate loss from the isolated retina. We found that taurine depressed the b-wave signal of frog retina, with the maximum effect occurring at a concentration of 1 mM. Addition of strychnine (0.4 mM) reversed the taurine effect on the b-wave, indicating that taurine receptors must be present in the inner retina. By contrast, taurine (0.1 – 20 mM) had no effect on the P111 component of the ERG initiated by either aspartate or cobalt. However, taurine exerted a modest depressant activity on P111 initiated by glutamate. The significance of these data relative to the putative neurotransmitter function of taurine in the inner retina is discussed.
[https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-4899-0117-0\_51](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007%2F978-1-4899-0117-0_51)

> **Summary**
> GABA content of isolated, dark adapted frog retina was found to be 3.15 ± 0.28 mM. After 30 minutes of exposure to intense light (200 1x), retinal GABA levels increased about 70%. Interestingly, incubation of dark adapted retina for 30 minutes with medium containing 0.4 mM taurine also led to a 70% increase in GABA levels. Since the light-induced elevation in GABA content was reduced over 50% by a simultaneous injection of 0.02 mM strychinine, it is likely that the light-induced GABA change is partly mediated by the release of taurine from the retina seen after light exposure. However, incubation of isolated retina with medium containing increasing concentrations of taurine (1, 2 and 20 mM), caused a progressive rise in 14C-GABA efflux from retina that was preloaded with 2.2 μM GABA and exposed to dim light (0.05 1x). It was also shown that taurine (1 and 5 mM) dramatically reduced 14C-aspartate efflux from retina preloaded with radioactive aspartate and exposed to dim light conditions. By comparison, intense light stimulation (40 1x) reduced basal 14C-aspartate efflux while dark exposure increased 14C-aspartate loss from the isolated retina. We found that taurine depressed the b-wave signal of frog retina, with the maximum effect occurring at a concentration of 1 mM. Addition of strychnine (0.4 mM) reversed the taurine effect on the b-wave, indicating that taurine receptors must be present in the inner retina. By contrast, taurine (0.1 – 20 mM) had no effect on the P111 component of the ERG initiated by either aspartate or cobalt. However, taurine exerted a modest depressant activity on P111 initiated by glutamate. The significance of these data relative to the putative neurotransmitter function of taurine in the inner retina is discussed.
> [https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-4899-0117-0\_51](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007%2F978-1-4899-0117-0_51)

### Da-mo — 2017-08-15T02:48:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/taurine-light-gaba-efflux-in-the-retina-this-explains-a-lot.20002/post-221672>

Id like to know too - especially with many of the energy drinks these days containing Taurine.

> **Scientists close in on taurine's activity in the brain (Red Bull drinkers, take note)**
> Taurine is one of the most plentiful amino acids in the human brain, but neuroscientists are still puzzled by just how brain cells put it to use. Now, a team of researchers at Weill Cornell Medical College (WCMC) has uncovered a prime site of activity for the molecule, bringing them closer to solving that mystery.
> "We have discovered that taurine is a strong activator of what are known as GABA \[gamma-aminobutyric acid\] receptors in a regulatory area of the brain called the thalamus," said study senior author Neil L. Harrison, professor of pharmacology and pharmacology in anesthesiology at WCMC. "We had discovered these receptors two years ago and showed that they interact with the neurotransmitter GABA -- the brain's key inhibitory transmitter -- that is also involved in brain development. It seems that taurine shares these receptors."
> The finding, reported in the January issue of the Journal of Neuroscience_,_ opens the door to better understanding taurine's impact on the brain.
> While the amino acid is made naturally by the body, it's also a much-touted ingredient in such so-called "energy drinks" as Red Bull. "Its inclusion in these supplements is a little puzzling, because our research would suggest ... \[that\] taurine actually would have more of a sedative effect on the brain," Harrison said.
> The prime focus of the study, however, was to find a site for the neurological activity of taurine.
> "Scientists have long questioned whether taurine might act on an as-yet-undiscovered receptor of its own," said lead researcher Fan Jia, postdoctoral scientist in anesthesiology. "But after some recent work in our lab, we ended up zeroing in on this population of GABA receptors in the thalamus."
> The thalamus, located deep in the brain's center, is involved in what neuroscientists call "behavioral state control," helping to regulate transitions between sleep and wakefulness, for example.
> "It's like a railway junction, controlling information traffic between the brainstem, the senses and the executive functions in the cortex," Harrison said. The researchers exposed thin slices of thalamic tissue from the brains of mice to concentrations of taurine that were similar to what might be found in the human brain.
> "We found that taurine is extraordinarily active on this population of GABA receptors in the thalamus," Harrison said. "It came as a bit of a surprise that the same receptor was used by both taurine and GABA. Nevertheless, finding taurine's receptor has been like discovering the 'missing link' in taurine biology."
> Now, Harrison's group is trying to determine what taurine actually does in the brain; Harrison said that because GABA is important for forging new cell-to-cell connections in the developing brain, "and because taurine shares a receptor with GABA, it, too, may play a role in neurological development."
> As for energy drinks, "Remarkably little is known about the effects of energy drinks on the brain," Harrison said. "Assuming that some of \[taurine\] does get absorbed, the taurine ... may actually play a role in the crash people often report after drinking these highly caffeinated beverages."
> The work was funded by the U.S. National Institutes of Health. Co-researchers include Minerva Yue, Angelo Keramidas and Peter A. Goldstein of WCMC as well as Dev Chandra and Gregg E. Homanics of the University of Pittsburgh.

[https://news.cornell.edu/stories/2008/02/scientists-close-taurines-activity-brain](https://news.cornell.edu/stories/2008/02/scientists-close-taurines-activity-brain)

### Da-mo — 2017-08-15T02:56:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/taurine-light-gaba-efflux-in-the-retina-this-explains-a-lot.20002/post-221673>

> **Effect of taurine and caffeine on sleep–wake activity in _Drosophila melanogaster_**
> [Fang Ju Lin](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Lin%20FJ%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23616711),1 [Michael M Pierce](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Pierce%20MM%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23616711),1 [Amita Sehgal](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Sehgal%20A%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23616711),2 [Tianyi Wu](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Wu%20T%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23616711),1 [Daniel C Skipper](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Skipper%20DC%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23616711),1 and [Radhika Chabba](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Chabba%20R%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23616711)1
> 1Department of Biology, Coastal Carolina University, Conway, SC, USA
> 2Department of Neuroscience, HHMI at University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, PA, USA
> Correspondence: Fang Ju Lin, Department of Biology, Coastal Carolina University, P.O. Box 261954, Conway, SC 29528, USA Tel +1 843 349 6484 Fax +1 843 349 2201 Email [Author information ► Copyright and License information ►](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#)
> [Copyright](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/about/copyright/) © 2010 Lin et al, publisher and licensee Dove Medical Press Ltd
> This is an Open Access article which permits unrestricted noncommercial use, provided the original work is properly cited.
> This article has been [cited by](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/citedby/) other articles in PMC.
> [Go to:](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#)
> **Abstract**
> Caffeine and taurine are two major neuromodulators present in large quantities in many popular energy drinks. We investigated their effects on sleep-wake control in constant darkness using the fruit fly _Drosophila_ as a model system. It has been shown that caffeine, as the most widely used psychostimulant, can boost arousal through the dopamine pathway in the mushroom bodies of flies. Taurine is a GABA receptor agonist, which is inhibitory to neuronal firing. We show here that flies receiving a low dose of caffeine (0.01%) increase locomotor activity by 25%, and decrease total sleep by 15%. Treatment with taurine at 0.1% to 1.5% reduces locomotor activity by 28% to 86%, and shifts it from diurnal to nocturnal. At 0.75%, taurine also increases total sleep by 50%. Our results show that taurine increases sleep, while caffeine, as previously reported, attenuates sleep. Flies treated with both caffeine and taurine exhibit two differential effects which depend upon the ratio of taurine to caffeine. A high taurine:caffeine ratio promotes sleep, while a low ratio of taurine:caffeine inhibits sleep to a greater extent than the equivalent amount of caffeine alone. This intriguing enhancement of caffeine action by low doses of taurine may account for the presence of both compounds in energy-promoting drinks such as Red Bull® and Monster®.
> **Keywords:** caffeine (trimethylxanthine), energy drinks, GABA (γ-aminobutyric acid), locomotor activity, sleep, taurine (2-aminoethanesulfonic acid)
> In the last decade, energy drinks such as Red Bull® and Monster® have become cultural icons and are frequently marketed towards and consumed by young adults. These drinks are intended to keep people awake at work or during leisure activities. Unfortunately the frequent consumption of these beverages has been linked to risky behavior among college students.[1](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b1-nss-2-221) Two common ingredients found in energy drinks are caffeine and taurine, both classified as neuromodulators. The lack of regulation of caffeine content that could lead to caffeine intoxication, and its recreational use in combination with alcohol, have become growing concerns (see review by Reissig).[2](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b2-nss-2-221) According to US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) regulations, the maximum caffeine content allowed is 71 mg/12 fl.oz. (or 0.02%),[3](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b3-nss-2-221) yet many energy drinks have clearly exceeded that limit for a single serving without any warning label. The amount of caffeine per serving in energy drinks ranges from 76–80 mg or 0.03% (eg, original Red Bull® or Monster®), equivalent to that in a single cup of coffee, to 280 mg or 0.11% (eg, Cocaine®) in 8.3 oz. or 250 ml. Monster XXL® has as much as 240 mg of caffeine in a 23.5 oz. can. The side effects of caffeine include insomnia, nausea, heart palpitations, and even death in rare cases. Caffeine is also known to delay the onset of sleep, shorten stage 3–4 nonrapid eye movement (NREM) sleep and reduce total sleep.[4](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b4-nss-2-221) In addition, taurine, a sulfhydryl amino acid found in many tissues including the CNS and muscles, is involved in osmotic regulation[5](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b5-nss-2-221) and in modulating the release of other amino acid transmitters at synapses.[6](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b6-nss-2-221) Reported benefits of taurine include cytoprotection[7](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b7-nss-2-221) and anti-seizure/epilepsy effects.[8](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b8-nss-2-221) Both Red Bull® and Monster® have 1000 mg or 0.4% of taurine in the original 250 ml packages, and the amount triples in Monster XXL®. Though manufacturers claim that caffeine and taurine are safe and beneficial, the interaction between caffeine and taurine has not fully been investigated.
> The fruit fly _Drosophila melanogaster_ serves as a useful model to study the sleep-wake cycle since it shares conserved mechanisms with mammals.[9](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b9-nss-2-221)–[11](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b11-nss-2-221) The circadian (~24 hour) clock that maintains the timing of the sleep-wake cycle in _Drosophila_ resides in the lateral neurons (LNs) of the brain. Within these neurons, a molecular clock maintained by the products of key clock genes: _period_ (_per_), _timeless_ (_tim_), _Clock_ (_Clk_), and _cycle_ (_cyc_), results in the rhythmic regulation of a neuropeptide, PDF (pigment-dispersing factor).[12](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b12-nss-2-221) In turn, PDF promotes arousal during the late night/early morning via PDF-responsive neurons that control downstream motor activity. A second process controlling the sleep-wake cycle, also known as homeostatic control of sleep, determines the amount of sleep rather than the timing of sleep. Results from the Hendricks and Shaw groups have demonstrated that the rest behavior _of Drosophila_ is independent of the circadian clock, and it exhibits similar characteristics to mammalian sleep behavior.[13](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b13-nss-2-221),[14](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b14-nss-2-221) Mushroom bodies (MBs), though best known for their function in learning and memory, are also involved in suppressing locomotion,[15](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b15-nss-2-221) and promoting sleep[16](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b16-nss-2-221),[17](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b17-nss-2-221) at least in part through serotonin signaling.[18](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b18-nss-2-221)
> Neurotransmitters such as dopamine, glutamate, norepinephrine, orexin, and serotonin are involved in promoting wakefulness in mammals,[19](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b19-nss-2-221) while adenosine along with other neuropeptides modulate sleep.[20](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b20-nss-2-221),[21](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b19-nss-2-221) Similar pathways are present in _Drosophila_, with arousal controlled by dopamine and octopamine,[22](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b22-nss-2-221),[23](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b23-nss-2-221) while sleep is promoted by the GABAergic pathway and the serotonin d5-HT1A receptor.[18](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b18-nss-2-221) GABA elicits a Cl− current and hyperpolarizes the cell membrane, hence it is generally considered to be an inhibitory neurotransmitter in _Drosophila_,[24](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b24-nss-2-221) although excitatory GABA receptors have also been identified in invertebrates.[25](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b25-nss-2-221),[26](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b26-nss-2-221) Similarly, it has been shown that taurine is a GABA/glycine receptor agonist,[27](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b27-nss-2-221)–[29](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b29-nss-2-221) though it requires much higher concentration. Agosto[30](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b30-nss-2-221) and Parisky[31](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b31-nss-2-221) were the first to link sleep behavior to GABA receptors in _Drosophila_, which were identified in the lateral neurons (LNs). Our specific aims in this study were to: 1) study the effect of taurine on sleep; and 2) examine the caffeine:taurine interaction involved in sleep–wake cycle control, at the same ratio found in the energy drinks.

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/)

### Da-mo — 2017-08-15T03:01:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/taurine-light-gaba-efflux-in-the-retina-this-explains-a-lot.20002/post-221674>

> **GABA & Glycine Receptor Modulation**
> While taurine fails to easily cross the blood-brain barrier, **_the compound engages a unique carrier-mediated transport system that delivers it across the blood-retinal barrier, essentially giving taurine backdoor access to nervous tissue._** Once there, taurine functions as an inhibitory neurotransmitter, calming brain activity by engaging the GABA & glycine receptors. Modulation of these brain systems reduces excess stimulation (THINK: anxiety & stress) while exerting anxiolytic relaxation.
> **Anti-Aging Neuroprotection**
> Taurine is often touted as an antioxidant, occasionally as an anti-inflammatory. This [study](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23963537) suggests that taurine’s neuroprotective role is an amalgamation of effects, particularly on the GABAergic & somatostatinergic (growth hormone inhibitor) system, with taurine altering measures of GABA & glutamate, neuropeptide somatostatin, hippocampal expression, and more. Through interaction with the GABAergic system, taurine supplementation is believed to help forestall age-related cognitive decline.

[http://nootropicgeek.com/taurine-review/](http://nootropicgeek.com/taurine-review/)

# Thread: Hello from Colorado Springs!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-colorado-springs.20003/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 35
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Outlier — 2017-08-13T06:24:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-colorado-springs.20003/post-221575>

While I have been following Jack for over 3 years, I have only just joined the forum as well as becoming a klub member. While I have learned so much and experienced success, the fact I am now dealing with sleep apnea and weight gain demonstrates without words that I don't understand enough.

I joined as a member because I was listening to a radio cast of Jack mentioning that in one of his bio hacks, he had given himself sleep apnea and it seemed it wasn't easy to get rid of. I am hoping to find more about that whole situation. In the mean time, I am trying to get my D3 between 60 and 80 (per Stasha Gominak) as well as raise B5. There's only one desire with this - I must have uninterrupted, non hypoxic, healing sleep.

I think my next step is cold thermogenesis. I had done this before and believe it to be the major contributor to turning around my health. However, when I tried it a few weeks or so back, the cold felt like burning. The water temp was only 55 degrees so I suspect I'm far more warm adapted than I was the first time I tried it. I used to easily sink into a tub of 45 to 50 degrees with ice and think it was comfy!!

So with a different set of problems to tackle, I am here to learn, draw the connections I've missed in the past, connect, share experiences and get my life back!!
\----------------------------------------------------
**Age:** 53
**Sex:** F
**Joined JackKruse.com:** 12 Aug 17
**Location:** Colorado Springs, CO USA
**Dx:** Complex Sleep Apnea; AHI 98
**Surgeries:** Gall Bladder removal and Tubal 1987; Tummy Tuck/Inner Thigh Lift 2009; Uterus removal 2013 (still have ovaries and cervix)
**Current Rx:** Lots of supplements (I obviously don't understand water, magnetism and mitochondria)
No prescription meds
Resmed Aircurve ASV with 2 lpm oxygen
**Current Therapies/Protocols:** Leptin Rx | Magnetico 20 gs |Red lights at night/blue light filters | Avoiding sensitive foods **Health WINS:** Previously lost 45lbs on Leptin Rx with cold thermogenesis
**Health Goals:** This sleep apnea MUST go. Sciatica pain must go. The fat must go. I want to practice kung fu again. I want my life back.
**My Optimal Journal:** No link yet; not started

### caroline — 2017-08-13T09:48:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-colorado-springs.20003/post-221580>

Dr. Kruse talks about red lights ..... are you doing this before bed?

How much outdoor time? grounding?

how much nnEMF is in your environment? work? home?

I would like to call you something more positive.....?

### Outlier — 2017-08-13T23:07:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-colorado-springs.20003/post-221607>

Thanks for the welcome, Caroline! I will be getting my journal going here soon - hopefully today.

I have not done a 23&me test. I wasn't sure what that was and just looked it up. If it'something I absolutely need, I can get it done. However, I am one of those people that refuse to participate in offerings of the insurance companies to provide metrics for discounts because I don't trust what happens to the information. While I know my personal information can be taken without my knowledge, I just prefer not to give it to anyone. My DNA info in particular is part of that information. Having said that though, I just gave away 26 vials of blood for a number of health tests. I will report the results back on my journal when I get them. I have a baseline from around 3 years ago when I first did the Leptin Rx with CT. I have another from June of 2016 and now the tests ordered 3 days ago. I should have results in the next week or two. It does not include salivary cortisol or DHEA, I'll have to do those separate.

I have read the CT protocol and admit by-passing the face dunking "because I used to do this and it shouldn't be that hard to get back to it." Yeah.  I was wrong!! I need to follow the protocol.

Yes, I'm doing the red lights in the evening after sunset when I would normally have regular lights on in the house. I have been photo-sensitive since my teen years and this certainly helps. I was already using lights dim enough that friends complained about me living in the dark and that they couldn't see to move around the house. The red lights are so much less invasive to my eyes. I use Twilight on my cell phone which is a 'follow the sun' type app that increases blue light filter depending on the time of day for your specific location. I have blu-blocker lenses in my glasses for when I use them. I've had lasik and have 20/15 at distance, but need one pair of glasses for intermediate distance (around 2 feet or so) and one pair for close up and reading. I can't do bifocals.

Outdoor time . . . certainly not enough due to sciatic pain. Without pain, I used to walk for at least 1 to 2 hours a day, and then hit the trail on the weekends for 4 hours each day. I can't even walk (or stand) 20 minutes without pain. When I get up in the mornings and take the dog out, we also walk to the OTHER side of the house where I can look into the sun. I've been doing this about 3 weeks or so. Also with that, I have been sitting in the sun for 20 to 30 minutes a day with my bare back exposed to the sun (usually around 11 am, but always between 10 am and 2 pm). Other than running the dog outside for potting breaks and letting him run and play, I don't get outside much. Being a fair-haired blonde, with very fair skin, being photo sensitive and having sciatic pain make for a difficult time for me being outside!! Also, my house is at about 7000 feet so I'm over a mile closer to the sun than my sea level friends. Bright light indeed!

Grounding . . . I could use some tips here. I sleep on a Magnetico. Not sure if that counts for grounding. I hate shoes (I grew up in a very rural area where we only wore shoes during school or going to town. We basically kicked off the shoes at the start of summer vacation and didn't put them back on til fall when school started.) Even now, I don't wear shoes - I'm barefoot all the time. When I'm outside in the back, 90% of the time I'm barefoot on concrete. The other 10%, I'm barefoot on rocks. I have natural prairie grass in my back yard which translates to others as 'lots of weeds'. In particular, many have burrs or other sharp edges so I don't go barefoot in that! There's nothing else I do that I can think of that may count towards grounding so we may have some wide opportunity for change here.

I'm not sure about nnEMF around me. I know there are 34 tower structures within a 4 mile radius and 144 antenna. I do plan on getting a trimeter and RF meter for Wifi and such here soon. I recognize that Jack recommends a professional evaluation. I will have to look into that when I can better afford it. I work from home so I do need to be aware of what is around me because it's constant. I have lived in this home for 17 years. However, I absolutely suspect something environmental has changed and is exerting a negative influence over my health. It may be some apartments built nearby. I have no baseline for comparison, so I will have to see what I can find. This is certainly a major area of concern and motivation behind becoming a Gold member. I AM considering moving, but then, I have no idea how to ensure that any different place is better.

You are welcome to call me Lisa as that is my name, but please don't think the nickname Outlier is a negative thing!! Of all the archetypes that I identify with, I am a Seeker at heart. I want to know the truth of a thing more than I want words of comfort or security. This set me apart from others at an early age and was my first hint of being an outlier. I have found through my life that I fit very few bell curves. Insurance companies provide medical treatment based on bell curves. I recall going to the doctor with a slight fever - about 99.5 degrees and was told statistically, I did NOT in fact have a fever nor could he document that in my record. I pointed out that my basal body temp is 97 degrees and this represents a 2.5 degree rise in temperature so FOR ME, this was a fever. While he acknowledged it, he said he still couldn't write it as such. Medical advice based on averages and bell curves has not helped me. I am on this site because I AM an outlier. I need help beyond the ordinary advice. I associate 'outlier' with thinking outside the box and having the courage and willingness to discover the truths that are there. I celebrate being an outlier because it represents freedom from everything average (or expected). Jack himself is an outlier and his teachings are outside the mainstream and backed by science that others cannot explain away no matter how much they don't want to know the truth. I'm proud to be an outlier and I think everyone that has joined this community has a measure of it them!!

### caroline — 2017-08-14T04:07:49+0300

### Outlier — 2017-08-14T07:36:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-colorado-springs.20003/post-221630>

It is possible for me to work outside. Having said that, Colorado Springs IS the land of crazy, severe weather. I am a project manager and spend most of my days on conference calls and on the computer. In planning to work outdoors, what are the goals? Do I want to spend more time in the sun? Is the goal to be grounded to the Earth? Being in the direct sun can make seeing a monitor difficult. However, I \*\* can \*\* pace around the yard like a caged lion while being on my calls. This is a habit I formed from my days of being in a little cube. What kind of footwear would be appropriate? Or, if I were to make a footpath, what could I use for it? Mulch? It's interesting that you asked me about this - as soon as my back eas healthy enough to support the activity, I was planning to practice my kung fu forms outside while taking my conference calls. Many of the forms, and anything chi kung in particular, teach grounding methods. While these days people think of that type of grounding as a person being hard to move, I'm certain its origins were to literally ground with and to the Earth.

I am generally outside in the mornings for just a short time for the specific purpose of looking towards the sun. But not grounded and not naked! Also, not exactly at sunrise. A question if you can assist? For at least the past year and maybe 3 years in total, I have not gotten healing sleep due to my apnea. I have only been on bipap then asv treatment a total of 3 months. I usually try to sleep as long as possible because for the past few years, that sleep has been deeply hypoxic. In one of my sleep studies, my oxygen sats were documented in the 60s. I personally have observed it in the 50s. Hours of nighttime hypoxia at these levels kill brain, heart, lung, kidney, liver cells, etc. Of my 3 months of treatment, I've only had the supplemental oxygen for the past 6 weeks. So my question is, where should my current priority lie? Should I short sleep to be up at sunrise because light is simply THAT important? Or should I recover more healing sleep and look at the sun when I get up? And just so you know, I usually get up between 5:30 and 6:30am on weekdays and sleep til 8, 9 or 10am on weekends.

I do not not have a kiniki swimsuit, but I think I need that adorable wrap!! I will be getting one soon!!

It's funny you asked about the Vermont talk. Earlier today, I was wondering what to listen to and settled for the current Aug 2017 webinar. The first thing I heard Jack say was now that people have had time to digest the Vermont discussion . . . so I stopped and looked up the Vermont talk. Yes, I listened to it in its entirety tonight but will have to listen several times to get everything.

I did walk away with the importance of light. However, I don't have enough understanding to answer my question above. Is light my priority or is healing sleep. I know I need both and at some point the answer will be that I need sunrise light more than sleep. But is that time now? What do I base that kind of decision on?

I also heard that if I get the 23&me testing done, it's possible Dr. K can advise me if I can heal in my current zipcode. If I get that testing done, do I need the $99 test or the $199 test?

Lastly, I've only read parts of the thread on why UV feels different in the morning - I will go back and read more.

In the Vermont talk, one of the points made was that we are made to be addicted to the sun. From my earliest recollections, the sun was never my favorite place to be. My mother always said I was 'allergic' to the sun. No doubt by my teen years, I avidly avoided the sun. I also begin to sleep excessively. Even now when I sit in the sun for 20 minutes, I can't wait to get out of it. Exercise has never brought any pleasure either. Suggests a dopamine issue, yeah? But why hasn't the light changed it?

### caroline — 2017-08-14T09:15:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-colorado-springs.20003/post-221632>

Hi Lisa - Those are a lot of big important questions ..... have you thought of having a consult with Jack?

You are doing the leptin reset? That is really about light and resetting your circadian rhythm.

### Outlier — 2017-08-14T22:25:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-colorado-springs.20003/post-221657>

I think eventually I will need to do a consult with Jack. Given how crazy busy he is, I was hoping to anticipate as many of his questions as possible and have information he needs at hand. At minimum, I'd like to get my labs back that I just had taken on Aug 10th. My guess is some things will be in this week but the whole set won't be until next week.

I had started the leptin reset, but given odd cravings, I can't say I'm doing a good job managing carbs. To be specific, I crave salt. I have coarse ground celtic sea salt and will eat the crystals straight. My favorite thing to eat is pecans, dried cranberries, 90% cocoa dark chocolate and lots of salt!! I often make a meal out of it. It suggests to me I have water balance issues. I don't know if it is outright salt, potassium or magnesium. This is concerning to me because it's a hugh part of the water/light/magnetism picture. I clearly see the symptom but don't know the appropriate path to follow.

So what changed? Wow, Caroline!! That's what I have been asking myself over and over. I have been a project manager or working in IT for aome 25 years now. Life on the phone and computer has been part of that picture for the whole time. I tend to point to 2 baselines. In 2009, I had cosmetic surgery. They told me that due to the anesthesia, they would be able to tell as I was coming out of it whether I had sleep apnea. At the time, I did not. In July 2013, I did the leptin reset with CT and lost 45 lbs with zero effort. I looked good and felt good. In fact, my avatar pic is from that time - so you can judge for yourself! I was still overweight but very pleased nonetheless. My HSCRP was 0.4 and I was also pleased with that. Because I felt so good, I started taking kung fu classes that Nov. In Feb, my sleep hygiene failed entirely. I met a friend and started hanging out. Their environment was not good for me. First, he lived in an apartment building. Second, he left his TV on 24x7. I was having a great time playing video games with him and next thing I knew, it'd be 2am and I had to get up at 5:30. So I would sleep at his place but he only had a one bedroom apartment with no couch. So I slept in his room. I could not sleep with the noise and light of the TV. Not to mention he DID have sleep apnea and his breathing and waking kept me up as well. This persisted from Feb 2014 til Jun 2014 when he moved in my house as my roommate. At least then I was able to sleep in my room and he in his. However, he continually woke me before I completed restful sleep and he kept my CNS stimulated CONSTANTLY. (Keep in mind he was my kung fu buddy so think Pink Panther and Kato. We were always sparring and surprising each other with attacks.) This guy LIVED for TV and even though I don't have a TV on my main level, he watched constantly (into the evening) on his laptop. I ended up watching it too. In Feb of 2014, the sciatic pain started and it's never stopped. I had to stop practicing kung fu by Aug 2014 because of intense pain. At that point, I was not able to go for walks and pretty much was never outside because I felt so miserable. By March 2016, I had over 6 months of excessive daytime sleepiness. In May 2016, my doctor tried thyroid supplements. I gained 10lbs in the first week and 50 over the course of the summer. He sent me to another doctor who suspected narcolepsy (because of presence of cataplexy). He sent me to a sleep study. This is where I got the news of complex sleep apnea with an AHI of 98. I never made it to the daytime MSLT because of that.

So when you ask about what changed, I suspect I gave myself sleep apnea when I started hanging out with my friend and disrupted all of my sleep. My guess is that the sciatic pain came on as I developed night time hypoxia since lack of oxygen causes issues in nerve signaling.

My friend moved out at the end of April (2017) and I began sleep apnea treatment in May.

Does that give a good summary of what's different? I need to add that as part of my journal - I think all those 'lifestyle' changes had a huge impact. We can absolutely quantify a disconnect from light over the past 3 years. If I understood enough of the Vermont discussion, this alone could be the issue. If I truly have narcolepsy, then I additionally have an orexin issue.

### Robert Marking — 2017-08-15T08:04:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-colorado-springs.20003/post-221684>

> [Outlier said:](/goto/post?id=221630)
>
> I also heard that if I get the 23&me testing done, it's possible Dr. K can advise me if I can heal in my current zipcode. If I get that testing done, do I need the $99 test or the $199 test?
>
> In the Vermont talk, one of the points made was that we are made to be addicted to the sun. From my earliest recollections, the sun was never my favorite place to be. My mother always said I was 'allergic' to the sun. No doubt by my teen years, I avidly avoided the sun. I also begin to sleep excessively. Even now when I sit in the sun for 20 minutes, I can't wait to get out of it. Exercise has never brought any pleasure either. Suggests a dopamine issue, yeah? But why hasn't the light changed it?

Hey welcome aboard, I am kinda new here myself. I believe all you need is the $99 test. What's great about that is you can download all the raw data and run it through other websites such as geneticgenie.com or promethease.com for basically a small donation of $5 and youll get extensive info on your DNA, genes, and genetic mutations.

It's funny that you say that being in the sun was never your favorite place and that you avoided the sun, and you began to sleep excessively. I have had that same issue majority of my lifetime. The sun takes a lot out of me. When I would be up early morning and stay inside, I am Ok, but if I'd walk out to see the sunrise, I'd get nauseous most of the time and I wouldn't feel well and I'd have to go back to bed. I never could figure that out. That's why I never like mornings, even as a kid. I never felt good in the morning sun, that's why I would sleep as late as I could, and I would stay up late night as well. People accused me of being a vampire at times.

Only recently, I discovered through evaluating my patients through Autonomic Response Testing, that many of them had a stress reaction so severe to exposure of the Sun, Moon, Magnetism (component parts of the photoelectric effect), that it caused their Autonomic Nervous System to push them into a Sympathetic (fight or flight) stress mode, which is not a good thing. Just to be certain, I had two other ART practitioners, including one top practitioner / instructor in Philly and another from Atlanta test their patients and they received the same results as well.

I am estimating that because of the patients sympathetic stress response that they are only receiving approx. 20% of the benefits of the photoelectric effect. Remember, 2/3 of your net negative electron charge should come from the Photoelectric Effect. I tested myself and of course I had the same issue. I have a feeling that this problem is very widespread and maybe a big contributor to a lot of health issues that people are having. Fortunately there is a way to clear this sensitivity.

> It's funny that you say that being in the sun was never your favorite place and that you avoided the sun, and you began to sleep excessively. I have had that same issue majority of my lifetime. The sun takes a lot out of me. When I would be up early morning and stay inside, I am Ok, but if I'd walk out to see the sunrise, I'd get nauseous most of the time and I wouldn't feel well and I'd have to go back to bed. I never could figure that out. That's why I never like mornings, even as a kid. I never felt good in the morning sun, that's why I would sleep as late as I could, and I would stay up late night as well. People accused me of being a vampire at times.
>
> Only recently, I discovered through evaluating my patients through Autonomic Response Testing, that many of them had a stress reaction so severe to exposure of the Sun, Moon, Magnetism (component parts of the photoelectric effect), that it caused their Autonomic Nervous System to push them into a Sympathetic (fight or flight) stress mode, which is not a good thing. Just to be certain, I had two other ART practitioners, including one top practitioner / instructor in Philly and another from Atlanta test their patients and they received the same results as well.
>
> I am estimating that because of the patients sympathetic stress response that they are only receiving approx. 20% of the benefits of the photoelectric effect. Remember, 2/3 of your net negative electron charge should come from the Photoelectric Effect. I tested myself and of course I had the same issue. I have a feeling that this problem is very widespread and maybe a big contributor to a lot of health issues that people are having. Fortunately there is a way to clear this sensitivity.

Robert, thanks for the great information and sharing your experiences in the sun. Certainly in my baby and preschool years when I had zero self control, if I got too hot from the sun, I turned into a little monster. No feel good hormones for baby Me at all!! It was like someone flipped a switch and I became quite hostile and down right mean and nasty. Fortunately, self control tempers that, but I honestly am still subject to that today. I grew up in Virginia where it is both hot and humid and that combination was bad for me. When my sister hit her teens, she suddenly started sleeping until 1 or 2 in the afternoon during the summers. It was hard because we used to go to the local swimming pool all day (I may not be much for the sun, but I've always been a Water Baby!) However, when I hit my teens, the same thing happened. Suddenly, normalcy was staying up until 2am and sleeping til noon or later. Even today, if work was not a consideration, I will slowly adjust back to going to bed at 2am and getting up at 10am. The only place I ever fit societal norms was in Hawaii. That's because 10pm there is 2am here and that's my 'real' bed time. And when it's my wake up time of 10am?? It's 6am there. So I went to bed and got up normally while there.

Being on Leptin Rx feels foreign. I try to get up at sunrise but I'm not rested. I look at the sun and it seemingly does nothing. I sit in the mid day sun and I burn and can't wait to get back inside.

So at a high level, I get the ART that you're doing. You said the issue is easy to clear but didnt say what it was. I'm assuming you wanted me to ask outright or Jack's protocols address it and it will 'self resolve' for lack of a better term. Do you care to elaborate?

### Outlier — 2017-08-15T22:01:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-colorado-springs.20003/post-221713>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=221686)
>
> good summary Lisa .....my guess - excessive nnEMF 24/7. and not much sunlight and grounding.
>
> What were you eating during that crazy period?
>
> Have you got all electronics out of your bedroom? Does your house have a smart meter? your neighbours?
>
> Maybe just start with face dunk, eatings lots of oysters etc., drinking great water
>
> watch the sunrise and sunset
>
> It sounds like you need a meter to test your environment .....Scompy and Drezy can help with that.

It's funny you ask what I was eating during that period!! When I first met my friend, I was Paleo/Leptin Rx. Having said that, I did not understand the difference between dietary ketosis and Jack's definition of ketosis. But generally a ton of salmon and cod with lots of coconut oil. However, my friend eats nothing but garbage. I'm talking worse than SAD here. The boy simply will not eat fresh food. It MUST be processed. Having him in my house certainly caused me to shift back towards SAD like eating. For example, he wouldn't eat the BEAUTIFUL, wild caught salmon I bought - it had icky skin and soft bones. So I then went to a wild caught salmon with no skin or bone - read 'processed' and he would eat it. However once my weight went insane on the thyroid meds, nothing in my body worked right. Id eat a quart of ice cream and LOSE 5 lbs. Only to eat a salad and gain 5 back. It made no sense and no matter what, I was on an upward trajectory. So I decided if I had to be fat and ugly, I was going to enjoy it!!! So I ate mostly sugar in the form of ice cream.

So let's take a quick look at what I think was happening. At the time, I was severely hypoxic at night. As in 50 to 60% saturation. This of course, killed my VO2 Max (and I haven't recovered that). So night times meant extended hypoxia with zero healing sleep. Daytime meant inefficient oxygen utilization. What does the biggest enery hog in my body need (meaning my brain)? It needs energy and lots of it. Without oxygen, where was that energy coming from? Glucose. Almost a pure and constant supply. I didn't think about what I was eating. I just reached for sugar every time my brain called for it. And thanks to my friend, after nearly 3 years of gluten free, I added that back too. Like I said, if I had to be fat and ugly, I was gonna enjoy the ride for whatever I could.

The only thing that has caused a partial weight loss was ditching all the foods I'm sensitive to. I lost 20lbs without effort doing that. But right now, I'm in a complete homostasis and one look at me and you don't need labs . . . you will immediately conclude I'm leptin resistant!!

Unless our fine community has suggestions on how to make oysters yummy, I don't know if I can do that. Wild caught salmon and cod are my staples and a preference if given one.

I have to say, eating 50g of protein first thing in the morning is HARD. I'm not hungry and it's the LAST thing I wanna do!! I dont really wake up or want to eat til around 10am. I can't imagine trying to eat 50g of protein worth of oysters first thing in the morning!!

So I'm on track with you in finding and eliminating the nnEMF. I don't have to eat anything icky for that!!! I will reach out to Scompy and Drezy for some advice on RF meters!!

In the meantime, I'm looking towards the sunrise in the mornings, getting 20 min of mid day sun, eating as clean as I can (given weirdo salt cravings) and spring water is literally the only thing I drink. No coffee, tea or anything else.

What was designed to be good for you, some how your body recognizes the vibrational blueprint of the offending food as a threat, and it mounts an immune response to that food.

This stress on the cells, causes the cells to emit incoherent light, negatively effecting cell to cell communications, and negatively impacting the regulation of the biophoton field, which regulates all the biochemistry in the body. Your Automonic nervous system ANS response by moving into sympethetic fight or flight mode, kicking in adrenaline, moving the majority of the blood flow to the muscles, heart, and brain for action.

**_"Certainly in my baby and preschool years when I had zero self control, if I got too hot from the sun, I turned into a little monster. No feel good hormones for baby Me at all!! It was like someone flipped a switch and I became quite hostile and down right mean and nasty."_**

### Outlier — 2017-08-15T22:57:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-colorado-springs.20003/post-221720>

I share mold allergy/sensitivity with you as well. Virginia is a very wet place and mold abounds. It wasn't until I moved to Colorado that I breathed through my nose for the first time. Here, it gets hot, but humidity is usually very low.

So now you've got me really curious. I have not done ART or muscle testing at all. However, the lastest book I've purchased is about identifing food allergies via muscle testing. The author's premise is that food sensitivities, identified via muscle testing, exist due to nutritional deficiencies. The whole idea was intriguing, so I bought the book.

But, that book was along similar lines of something I was already doing. Specifically, back in 1956, Arthur F. Coca wrote a book declaring he had the solution for obesity, diabetes, high blood pressure etc. His premise is that when you eleminate foods you are sensitive to, you recover health. (He didn't know Jack to get the bigger picture!!) His methodology for measuring sensitivity was pulse rate. I eleminated a lot of foods from my diet in this way. Some foods cause my heart rate to decrease. Some cause an immediate spike ot 20 bpm.

So what you've got me wondering is, if I take my pulse when I go in the sun, will I see a significant jump in heart rate? According to Coca, a jump of 5bpm is OK. Anything more is not. Please realize, I'm telling you the condensed version not his whole protocol.

I will follow up with you tomorrow mid day for me and let you know what I find!! We're just about out of optimal range for the sun and about to get afternoon showers.

### Robert Marking — 2017-08-15T23:44:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-colorado-springs.20003/post-221726>

Regarding muscle testing at home, You could try this,

first thing in the morning, stand tall put your out arm out at a 90° angle in front of you, so its horizontal with a floor, and have someone gently push down on your wrist to check your strength levels in that arm, repeat that for a five times just to get a gauge of how strong your arm is. The person pushing down on your wrist should try to be consistent in his pressure as you are being consistent in your resistance.

Now you should take your free hand and totally cover your ear, pressing the ear inward with the palm of your hand.

Now With one hand over your ear and with one arm out , have the person press on your wrist again while doing this.

1) If your arm goes weak that you are and parasympathetic mode and you are testable at this moment.

2) If your arm stays the same strength as it was before you put your hand over the ear , Then that means that some type of hidden stressor has pushed you into sympathetic mode , and your autonomic nervous system is not testable with muscle testing.

However If your arm goes weak with your hand over your ear, that means you are testable.

So here's what you do next.

Well still indoors, Take your hand away from your ear, and have the person push down in your wrist to check your strength levels again, you should be strong once again.

then walk outside in the direct sunlight and repeat the test. If you're strong muscle goes weak in the sun, that means that the how do I not make nervous system is having a stress response (sensitivity.)

If you're strong muscle stay strong, or possibly even goes stronger, That may mean that you are having a strong sympathetic reaction to the sunlight, the adrenals are kicking in adrenaline, and youve moved into fight or flight mode,

To see if this is the case , While still standing in the sun, , with your arm out to be tested, you put your hand over your ear one more time ,

1) if your arm stays strong , that means your autonomic nervous system is highly stressed and has moved into sympathetic dominance fight or flight mode , Which is more of an allergic reaction than a sensitivity.

2) your arm goes week, and that means that you are in parasympathetic mode and the sun is not a stressor

I know it may be a little confusing but this is a great way to tell if something is a stressor or not, and to what degree it is a stressor whether it's a sensitivity or more of an allergy.

### Robert Marking — 2017-08-16T05:22:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-colorado-springs.20003/post-221745>

> [Karlos Amigos said:](/goto/post?id=221737)
>
> So even though I don't enjoy consuming it the way I would steak, it's become a favorite meal for the fact that I get a very noticeable buzz from them (raw only)

If you get a buzz from any food, that could be a result of the body having an immune response to that food, The autonomic nervous system sees this stress and it moves into a sympathetic fight or flight mode, Causing an adrenaline rush/buz.

The bigger buzz that you get from a food , means The stronger immune response that you'll have to the food, The more stress and inflammation that food Is causing, and more adrenaline is released while in sympathetic dominance mode to fight that food .

So typically whatever foods you crave the most, or the foods that you have the hardest time getting up, or the food that you love the most, are the foods that are causing the most amount of inflammation and stress in the body.

You could do the Dr. Coca test before and after eating any food to see if you have a sensitivity or allergy to it .

It's really all about what your immune system and white blood cells have to say about the food you eating. There could be an immune response of some type on a very small scale causing inflammation and stress, but consciuosly you wouldn't feel much difference. But that doesn't mean the inflammation is it leading to some type of chronic illness.

Usually when people say "wow that food makes me feel good " or " I like the way I feel when I have that food" or "that's my comfort food" many times it's because of the slight adrenaline release from the ANS kicking into the fight or flight sympathetic mode.

The hardest food for people to give up is typically the food thats causing the most amount of inflammation, because of this euphoric adrenaline release, much like when you get off a roller coaster ride at a theme park.

The reason why a person cannot be muscle tested accurately in sympathetic mode is because in this state , the stressor (in this case food sensitivities, ) is causing The cells to emit incoherent light signaling , causing chaos throughout the biophoton feild, , which is kind of like your computer freezing up and you're not able to load and unload programs.

If a patient is tested while in sympathetic mode, the feedback from the autonomic nervous system is not going to be accurate and you'll receive a lot of false positive and false negatives.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-08-13T16:46:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-light-damaging-to-eyes.20004/post-221593>

The flip side of this question for me is more illuminating........is wearing sunglasses during the day when red and purple light are out doing their things on the regeneration pathways?

It can be DEADLY.

**SUNGLASSES:** Still think sunglasses are OK? Possible carcinogenic effects from filtering natural light were found accidentally in a conversation Dr. John Ott had with Dr. Albert Schweitzer’s daughter. The conversation pertained to her experiences with her father at Lambarene, on the west coast of Africa, and the rate of cancer found among those people. She said that when her father had started the hospital there in Africa, there was no cancer at all, but now it was a problem. She said that their simple surroundings had not changed or modernized.

The only thing different was that natives **_all wore sunglasses as a status symbol of the civilized world._** They could always be found wearing them “even when paddling their dugout canoes wearing no more than a loin cloth. Sunglasses were so prized that these new adornments carried a higher barter value than beads or other trinkets. Think about how the light of the sun is filtered by sunglasses. Something so small has a massive effect and we keep missing it.

When we have desynchronization of the SCN from the peripheral organ clocks, we call this a [phase shift in chronobiology.](http://web.archive.org/web/20081012230048/http://biology4.wustl.edu/faculty/herzog/JohnsonChronobioInt.pdf) This is how we lose track of Quantum time (small time) that I mentioned in [EMF 6](http://www.jackkruse.com/emf-6-quantum-time/). Moreover, this gets translated directly to leptin receptor signaling in the hypothalamus and downstream it directly affects all the hormones of life. It also is sensed in the pineal gland. The more blue light it gets the more calcium it leaches and the less melatonin is released. This ruins autophagy efficiency in humans and regeneration potential drops. Many modern humans now suffer from calcified pineal glands early in life-based upon CT scanning data. The leptin receptor is part of the ancient timing mechanism that life built to tell time. It gets signals from the analog and the digital system in the brain. This is why leptin is found in the biochemistry of animals that date back to the Cambrian explosion. **The leptin receptor does this by counting quanta of light, phonons, protons, and electrons from food.** **It is a quantum clock, not a calorie clock that most believe.**

It is intimately tied to energy balance and conservation and accurate timing of all biochemical reactions in the body. It works on the principle stated in Einstein's fourth paper from his miracle year in 1905. This paper is least well known of all four but the most cited for real life explanations of how the world really works. This paper was on the Brownian motion but it put forth a foundational principle in all of nature. **Any fluctuation in timing leads to a massive loss of energy and information.** This theorem is called the fluctuation-dissipation theorem. As I mentioned in my recent podcast with Ben Greenfield this theorem uses stochastic calculus and not ordinary calculus. Most humans think change is linear. It is not. It works on stochastic math, which is non-linear. This is why the stock market and human behavior vary. The variance is locked into a strict mathematic relationship. **That variance uses the square root relation.**

Unlike regular calculus, which is used to predict trajectories of ordinary motions like a ball, stochastic calculus inherently assumes this square root dependence on time. This is precisely why people do not get the changes they expect from measure to lose weight or reverse and illness. It is not a linear progression as they expect. I have said this numerous times on my forum, but few people have questioned me why. Now they know. It is based upon stochastic math and probability waves founded in Einstein's fourth paper. Most of life is lived in the tail of a bell curve and not under the curve. This offends common wisdom and that is why it invokes the Dunning Kruger effect. It is quite random on the very short time scales used in biology on the inner mitochondrial membrane where quantum tunneling acts. People think that time is linear. Neils Bohr wrote early in the 20th century that all life is complimentary. It can and must be thought of in two ways, one based upon a particle form and another based upon a waveform. It seems counter-intuitive, but that is precisely what Quantum mechanics requires of natures laws.

In quantum mechanics, time can only move forward or into the future. There is no chance to go back in time. Einstein's fourth foundational paper shows this, as a foundational physical law of the universe. Obesity researchers do not realize this because they are caught in the "wrong scale" of understanding. Biochemical reactions are **5 levels below this quantum level of understanding**. To build a building would you begin on the 5th floor before you started in the basement? Well, in my opinion not understanding QED is exactly why obesity researchers and modern man are clueless about obesity causation, variables controlling weight loss and the development of all neolithic diseases. We need to look at nature's laws to see how the foundation of how life works. **It is a quantum world and all life uses a quantum blueprint.** This means you need to know about electrons, protons, light, sounds, and isotopes. A primal blueprint is about 50 stories away from the basement. This is the math of the leptin receptor. It is a quantum thermostat that uses stochastic calculus to accurately assess the Joules in a system to measure energy balance over a quantum time scale.

This is how we can get leptin resistance in the brain, liver and muscles levels and this is why each one of these situations was given its very own blog in the original leptin series. That is way too complex for any beginner, especially an obesity researcher whose dogma blinds them to an inconvenient truth. ### drezy — 2018-06-22T21:12:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-light-damaging-to-eyes.20004/post-244085>

I've been running my own N=1(or 3) (rural) dark house at night for about a year. I operate one single red led (no a panel) in each bathroom because it sure is nice when my son "hits the target".

You get used to it. I've read some security articles on how it's safer in case of a break-in since you yourself know your home layout way better than any thief.

Full dark even fixed my wife's habit of leaving shoes smack dab in the middle of a hallway. If there are trip hazards in your house then it's just good sense to clean up.

Dark, sun baking, and cold turned around my \*forever\* insomniac wife's sleep. I honestly used to avoid my wife every morning because she was always miserable. Now every morning we sit outside watch the sunrise and talk about our plans together.

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-22T21:37:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-light-damaging-to-eyes.20004/post-244089>

My n=1 is no light to get to the bathroom after I've gone to bed. After an unfortunate accident heading for the bathroom in the dark of winter, misjudging the bathroom door, going into the office next to it instead and falling over a box of books, I had to rethink how I did it. I did consider running a rope along the side of the corridor to make sure I got to the right door, but opted instead to practice feeling my way round the bed, reach out and find the bedroom door, and count steps and find landmarks like a picture on the wall etc. Then did the same in reverse coming back. As my hack continued I found I very rarely need to get up to pee. DH wont go that far, but for me he does have a torch covered with a blue blocking gel filter, he could get outside the bedroom door OK, and makes sure the bedroom door is shut before turning the torch on, and turned the torch off before opening the door to come back to bed.

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-23T11:32:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-light-damaging-to-eyes.20004/post-244129>

> [Phosphene said:](/goto/post?id=244073)
>
> Yes and I’m still confused as to whether this includes moon, star and fire light.

Just musings but as we evolved under moon and starlight, I don't see it in the same way as fire. Lyall Watson's book Supernature. _"The Hughes Aircraft Laboratory in California has developed a "tilt meter" so sensitive that it has been able to record lunar tides in a cup of tea._" This implies to me that we don't even need to see the moon for it to affect the water in our bodies. (Important to hibernating animals?) A lunar influence on the tides of the water our cells make could potentially influence our physiology and behaviour. I'm post menopausal so can't do a hack as to a sync of my menstrual cycle, but my n=1 is that improving my redox has meant that my sleep doesn't suck at full moon, and I don't wake up in the night thirsty.

I was watching a programme about a film maker trying to film the biggest migration on earth - the nightly journey of the creatures at the bottom of the ocean who come to the surface at night to feed at a depth influenced by photosynthesis. He was having no luck at depths he could reach, and was wondering why, and I saw behind him was a full moon …. So he goes off to a research centre for deep ocean biology and got his answer … The deep ocean creatures he was hoping to film came up to where the light penetrating the water was at one per cent, so when the moon was full that one per cent was very much deeper (and their diet would presumably have been different at that depth?). He went back to the same spot when there was minimal moonlight, and got his shots. At the moment I'm not naturally getting any complete darkness at night, in December from the end of astronomical twilight in the evening to the time it begins in the morning is 12 hours. So this has me wondering if the under sea creatures have "seasons" dictated by the addition or subtraction of true darkness and moonlight?

I'm not a fan of candles or firelight at night, but that may be because I see phosphenes in the dark, and behind closed lids. Perhaps that makes me more sensitive to even candle flicker than most ….? But if I was camping out and a fire kept snakes away, I'd be first there with the matches … In paleolithic times it could have prevented freezing to death but it could also attract unwanted attention … Another programme I watched was on a man surviving the wilderness with freezing cold nights. He dug a sleeping pit in the ground, and lit a fire. As it started getting dark he placed flat rocks on the embers of the fire, got in and covered himself over with skins. The rocks emitted warmth from underneath, and he slept cosy under the stars. Without bears or snakes, under ambient light courtesy of mother nature, that's a hack I'd consider.

### Phosphene — 2018-06-23T18:34:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-light-damaging-to-eyes.20004/post-244168>

I ‘liked’ several of the posts above because I can sense that lights (completely) out after sunset is where this is headed, but I don’t really like it one bit.

I can sense this is going to morph into a personal rant so I’m going to transfer the rest of my thoughts to my journal.

My question though is, how do people with older kids REALISTICALLY expect to enforce this? Baby steps like everything else?

### Jenelle — 2018-06-23T21:01:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-light-damaging-to-eyes.20004/post-244182>

> [Phosphene said:](/goto/post?id=244168)
>
> I ‘liked’ several of the posts above because I can sense that lights (completely) out after sunset is where this is headed, but I don’t really like it one bit.
>
> I can sense this is going to morph into a personal rant so I’m going to transfer the rest of my thoughts to my journal.
>
> My question though is, how do people with older kids REALISTICALLY expect to enforce this? Baby steps like everything else?

As the sun heads down, we are winding down. That includes me going around and keeping the lights as minimal as possible (which shouldn't be on to begin with).

If there are still lights on in the house and it is dark outside ~ the blue blockers go on.

If we are all up too late due to the kids' late baseball games etc ~ we just get to bed as soon as we can.

If my 17 year old has to work until 10:00 PM at his restaurant job ~ which may happen a couple of nights per week ~ he may come home to a house of sleeping people. And he goes to bed. Or if I'm still up for some reason, we talk VERY briefly, and I say, "Let's talk about it while we watch the sunrise tomorrow."

Yes, I think it has to be baby steps. The good thing is ~ my family "gets it". So it has been a natural (not forced) progression.

The other good news is that every effort made turns out to be 100% worth it, because you see the difference in mood, energy, and motivation of all involved.
