# Thread: Afternoon Crying = Chronobiology?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### brent-next — 2017-10-20T02:02:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-226246>

I have a wonderful baby boy, born August 4, 2017. His mother is very devoted and attentive.

All through the morning he is just sweetness and light, at night he has minimal wake ups and only makes small noises, but in the afternoon, he cries and cries like something is really wrong. His mother is just as attentive and caring in the morning as she is in the afternoon.

Mom CAN eventually calm him down, but it seems like a daily 2 or 3 hour crying session is really unnecessary.

Any wisdom that you can dispense would be appreciated.

### ScottishEmma — 2017-10-20T09:52:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-226258>

Sounds like colic. My boy had it for the first three months. It was torture. Google 'The Witching Hour'. It started at night and crept earlier and earlier in the day. I've heard stories of it happening at different times.

He'll outgrow it soon. Hang in there. It's tough!!!

### Jack Kruse — 2017-10-20T18:19:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-226283>

It can be many things but one thing to consider is that sensory processing disorders often present this way. FYI.

### shiran — 2017-10-21T08:20:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-226324>

> [brent-next said:](/goto/post?id=226246)
>
> I have a wonderful baby boy, born August 4, 2017. His mother is very devoted and attentive.
>
> All through the morning he is just sweetness and light, at night he has minimal wake ups and only makes small noises, but in the afternoon, he cries and cries like something is really wrong. His mother is just as attentive and caring in the morning as she is in the afternoon.
>
> Mom CAN eventually calm him down, but it seems like a daily 2 or 3 hour crying session is really unnecessary.
>
> Any wisdom that you can dispense would be appreciated.

Had the same experience with the first two kiddos
My third one is going to be a biohacking mito kiddo I'll keep you updated... hhhh

I suggest you get a lot of patience it will pass , then try to check what happened in days the baby spends time in the sun or outside in general.. dose it affect the situation...

### ScottishEmma — 2017-10-23T06:47:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-226420>

Yes, I meant to add. The days where I held my boy ALL DAY (carried and held by someone if not me) the colic was definitely less. And I put him to bed super early and that also helped.

It will pass. It is hellish. One day he will stop doing it. Don't worry about it, it's very, very common!

### Allin — 2017-10-25T02:30:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-226520>

> [brent-next said:](/goto/post?id=226246)
>
> I have a wonderful baby boy, born August 4, 2017. His mother is very devoted and attentive.
>
> All through the morning he is just sweetness and light, at night he has minimal wake ups and only makes small noises, but in the afternoon, he cries and cries like something is really wrong. His mother is just as attentive and caring in the morning as she is in the afternoon.
>
> Mom CAN eventually calm him down, but it seems like a daily 2 or 3 hour crying session is really unnecessary.
>
> Any wisdom that you can dispense would be appreciated.

_My younger brother did this until my mother started feeding him baby cereal when he was about a week old. She said he was still hungry. Now this was back in 1954..._

### brent-next — 2017-10-25T23:52:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-226575>

Thanks to all for the suggestions.

We just moved to a different house, with an easier place for mom to take him out in the sun and less measured non native EMF.

Does anyone have any ideas how much a northern european baby boy should be out in 21 degree north tropical sun? Obviously it needs to be divided doses over the day.

There definitely has been less crying here in Mexico than there was in Costa Rica, and Mexico at this time of year has a lot more hours of sunshine.

### brent-next — 2017-11-01T19:04:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-227111>

My current theory is that my baby cries in the afternoon on days when he hasn't had enough sleep. During the first seven weeks of his life he would sleep through a construction site next to his bed, but at about seven weeks he became a lot more sensitive to sounds.

On days when he gets good morning and afternoon naps , we have very little afternoon crying.

Do any of you experienced parents have suggestions on getting babies to go asleep and stay asleep long enough to wake up feeling rested?

### drezy — 2017-11-01T20:26:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-227122>

FWIW I've had coworkers with the same issue.

If I had it all to do over again I'd experiment with outdoor walks with some sun exposure prior to naps. I know that when I get my NO dermal hit I feel very calm.

### brent-next — 2017-11-02T17:15:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-227177>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=227122)
>
> FWIW I've had coworkers with the same issue.
>
> If I had it all to do over again I'd experiment with outdoor walks with some sun exposure prior to naps. I know that when I get my NO dermal hit I feel very calm.

I've been at a loss to figure out how much sun to expose him to. He's a pasty white northern european baby and I don't want to burn him and I also know that he does need some sun, probably in divided doses over the day and before naps would be good.

### drezy — 2017-11-02T18:03:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-227186>

That AM non-burning sun might be pretty useful to leverage. My son and I built up a great solar callous using AM sun. So much so I told a blonde indoorsy buddy that I think I'd feel safe even in Columbia with him at my side. Relative to my new bronzed look he looks like he'd fetch a way bigger ransom and there'd be no point in abducting me.

### brent-next — 2017-11-04T20:52:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-227360>

Thanks. Here in Chuburna Mexico, the morning sun until about 9:00 a.. is strong but doesn't have that much UV in it. Might be a good idea. Thanks.

### brent-next — 2017-11-15T19:32:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-228077>

The end result seemed to be that the crying in the afternoon was due to lack of sleep. During the first seven weeks of his life he would sleep through anything, but after that we had to work doing the usual parenting tricks (walking and chanting) to get him to sleep enough in the morning and the afternoon.

When he gets his long morning and afternoon long naps there is minimal crying. If he doesn't get his long morning and afternoon naps he will cry much more in the afternoon.

Thanks to all.

### ScottishEmma — 2017-11-16T16:57:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-228190>

Welcome to parenthood. Just when you've got it figured out, he'll change. #kids

### drezy — 2017-11-16T17:11:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afternoon-crying-chronobiology.20315/post-228194>

Yeah brent never underestimate the fact that your child has two extremely finely tuned quantum sensors:

1.  Dad seems to be too relaxed
2.  Dad seems to think he'll be getting special access to mom tonight

Jim Al-Khalili's quantum robins are tricky but nothing tops those two sensors your baby has.

# Thread: Michael Leger - Opti-journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/michael-leger-opti-journal.20324/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 29
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael Leger — 2017-10-24T17:20:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/michael-leger-opti-journal.20324/post-226484>

First time posting here. By way of introduction:

I'm 61. I've been a life long athlete - too much for my own good. I began regular athletics when I was 16-17, martial arts primarily with lots of running and cycling. From 22-32 became vegetarian and worked out 2-3 hours everyday, some days as long as 6 hours. I did not see the regular results I was hoping for in my running so I worked out harder. Big mistake. I also did a lot of fasting during that decade of my life - 2 three-week fasts, stopped because I began breaking down muscle tissue - several 2 week and 1 week fasts and innumerable 3 day fasts. Also a big mistake. My recovery times were getting longer and longer and I didn't interpret the signal correctly and worked out even longer and more frequently. Began a very active interest in supplements trying to improve my performance, working in the field professionally as a vitamin-buyer at a large health food store in Chicago, then as a sales rep for a couple of very high quality supplement companies.

Gave up vegetarianism when my pregnant wife and I passed a burger king and it smelled like heaven. Never looked back, though I do have a life-long love of vegetables and a tremendous appreciation for food generally.

Maybe somewhat typical stressful lifestyle after the crazy martial arts period - full time or more than full time school with part-time to full time work. Out of school then full time and part time job. Was in software development for about 15 years - 55-100 hour weeks during that time period. And also trained very hard during various points in time as a reasonably successful amateur competitive cyclist. I've been a year-round cyclist for quite some time. Started out with a paper route in my early teens; Chicago winters are a bitch on a bike.

Hit a major wall towards the end of my software development career - physically, emotionally, mentally and spiritually. That was 10 years ago. An opportunity opened up for me to return to the health field - working for a company that makes products similar to homeopathics. Left that firm 7 years ago and started my own - Homeodynamics. During that time period, I finally realized that I have the recovery abilities of a pygmy pony. Stopped competitive cycling, and then began getting heart palpitations whenever I would workout to any level of strenuousness. Stopped exercising in any traditional sense and just did easy walks.
I've been walking barefoot, whenever the weather allows, for quite awhile, typically early morning with my dogs. I don't remember how I came across Jack Kruse's work a month or so ago, but he (your) work really caught my attention. I've started cold thermogenesis, loosely following the protocol. I am face dunking and I go out in the morning for 10 or 15 minutes in shorts. It's getting cold here and when it's rainy too. Instead of typical clothes walking my dogs, I'll wear less, down to a t-shirt and shorts if I can manage it, barefoot down to about 40f, painful below that. I've ordered the epi-paleo book and will take a careful look there.

I am very excited to be a part of this group. The ideas that I have come across while dancing through the materials presented are really lighting me up, so to speak.

### Michael Leger — 2017-10-25T17:26:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/michael-leger-opti-journal.20324/post-226545>

Thank you for the welcomes!

Today - walked dogs in t-shirt 35f for 20 minutes at 6:30a. 10mins shorts-only tai chi at 8:30a 35f. Finishing cold shower 30secs. ~50gr protein (powder in smoothie) ~3tbls coconut oil, dulse, hemp 'milk', kombucha, some frozen berries.

Very poor sleep last night. Woke up frequently and had a hard time going back to sleep. I have kept a dark bedroom for 2 years. I go to bed early ~8p. If I do not eat a full dinner, typically around 6p, I sleep very poorly. Would love to get to the point where I did not need to eat within a couple of hours of going to sleep.

More history - 2017 has been very eventful. I had a mini-stroke late April - I'm not sure it was a genuine stroke. I had a bit of numbness in my right leg. No residuals of any kind, walking, talking, thinking fine while in hospital stay. Had head and neck MRI/A. Two 'very tiny' lesions were found. But an odd image turned up in my neck. Vascular surgeon recommended surgery for a 'very small' blockage. When he opened my neck up, he found the right exterior carotid artery had a 90 degree kink and a 180 degree twist. Corrected. Personally I think the stroke symptoms were my body's way of getting me to someone who could correct what might have turned into a fatal problem. The stroke symptoms were also a serious warning sign that I have been pushing too hard. I started a successful business seven years ago and have been giving it all I've got. Then we had a routine visit from the FDA inspectors. Responding to their observations was and is an ordeal - in the classical sense of that word. At the same time, a close family member received a cancer diagnosis. Fortunately very early stage and we are addressing it very well.

### Michael Leger — 2017-10-26T16:05:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/michael-leger-opti-journal.20324/post-226647>

Sleep last night was OK. Used EM-Destress and Sleepy to get to sleep and get back to sleep.

I purchased a used Magnetico pad a few weeks ago. Testing it with a passive-type gauss meter, it registered barely 1 gauss at an 8" distance. It was advertised as a Core model, 17 years old. I'm reasonably certain it's a classic. Running the meter over the surface, I noted quite a few magnets placed incorrectly - + side up - and some of the magnets had clumped together around the head area. I like to diy stuff, so I ordered 400 magnets of the type that the magnetico company uses. They are somewhat weak magnets - if you place one magnet directly over a magnet on the surface of the foam, it will not be attracted to the magnet inside of the pad, or will only weakly be attracted. I decided to slice open the pad of every other magnet and placed 3 of the ceramic magnets over the open slice magnet. This increased the gauss reading at 8" to ~5 gauss. I will probably add 100 Nd magnets to boost the field above 10 gauss.

25 min. walk with the dogs at 6:45, 35f, T-shirt, shorts and moccasins. 13 mins tai chi at 7:35, shorts and moccasins only. Need to add gloves. Hands get too cold. Legs no issues. Upper body felt good. Breakfast, 5 eggs in oatmeal base with spagyric tumeric, tumeric powder, black pepper, ginger and salt.

### Michael Leger — 2017-10-29T20:25:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/michael-leger-opti-journal.20324/post-226888>

Sleep was mediocre. Wound up feeling would not relent.
Did a Sweat Lodge yesterday. Ambient temps were in the low 30s. The Lodge was very hot right away, but didn't stay hot the whole time. My adaptation to them has been quite good, but this one was even better. I\_think\_the cold thermogenesis has something to do with it. After the Lodge, I stood outside for 10 minutes soaking up some sun, then took a cold shower. The Lodge probably lasted about 1.5 hours. Felt great after the shower and cool air. Had a big breakfast about 5 hours before the Lodge, might have had something to do with ease of form too.

Sleep last night was pretty good. Today, 29f, walked the dogs for 25 mins right around sunrise. T-shirt, shorts, gloves and moccasins. Went back outside for more sun - shorts, gloves, moccasins. Did tai chi for 21 mins. Felt good, but will need to add some socks to the moccasins for this temp and lower. 50+ grams of protein powder, pluse ~3 tbls of coconut oil in this morning's smoothie. Still very full 4 hours later.

Ate clams for breakfast yesterday, cracked a tooth on a shell.
Taking 1tsp fermented cod liver oil daily, with 1000 IU Vit. D and 2 Omega 3 capsules.

Did 20 mins of Tai Chi, shorts, gloves, moccasins, yesterday 8a. Sunny. Temps 35f, along with a 25 min dog walk, t-shirt, shorts, gloves, moccasins at 6:30a.
Did 15 mins of Tai Chi today, 8a. Cloudy 32f, along with 25 min dog walk, ditto above clothing.

Had a hard time staying warm yesterday. Cold front moved in and it was very windy. Going to try some calisthenics this morning after the outside 'sun' light.

Breakfast today - 2 slices gluten free bread with lots of sprouted pumpkin seed butter and a little homemade sour cherry preserve.

### Michael Leger — 2017-11-01T16:22:57+0200

Sleep mediocre last night - an awful lot going on right now.

Emerald LM2, one shake.

Walked the dogs at 6:15. It's dark for the whole walk now. Temp 32f. Wore shirt and pants for all but the last 5 mins. Last 5 mins wore pants and moccasins only. Felt very good. Bracing.

Breakfast at 7:00, a little less than 1/2lb of ground lamb w/oregano, marjoram, thyme, lemon, larger serving of steamed broccoli 2 eggs, sauerkraut.

Tai chi at 8, very cloudy, temp 33f. Shorts, moccasins, gloves. 16 mins. Much easier to tolerate the cold with a belly full of big protein and fat. Last 30 secs of shower full cold. Feel very good now.

### Michael Leger — 2017-11-29T21:11:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/michael-leger-opti-journal.20324/post-229148>

Had issues with logging in. Catching up:

I've been consistently doing cold adaptation - walking my dogs around sunrise, shorts, gloves, moccassins. Temps have been as low as 9, as high as upper 30s. Then I do tai chi usually right at sunrise for about 15 minutes. If I'm taking a shower, I can take a full cold shower now. It's not easy, but I don't feel like I want to jump the hell out of the shower now. I usually follow up with cold water or a cold smoothie and stay lightly dressed.

I'm eating quite a bit more seafood. Typically every day, sometimes 2x/day. Oysters when they're fresh and accessible in the Northern mid-west. Lots of frozen shrimp, squid, mussels, octopus, fresh fish depending on availability. Titrating carbs. I need to lose about 10 lbs, and severe carb restriction increases stress levels, so I do not attempt full ketosis, not sure it's necessary in my case.

Sleep is still variable. Sometimes good, sometimes not so good. Work has been very busy, adding stress.

The type of meditation I do is called 'incubation'. It was described by the 'pre-Socratic' Greeks. In quotes because it's only true chronologically. Parmenides and Empedocles were very very different people than the Athenians. Basically incubation is: lay down, close your eyes, die. Search on Peter Kingsley and incubation for more info. This meditation form is indigenous to the West.

Working my way towards labs.

### Michael Leger — 2017-12-04T21:46:25+0200

Warm morning, relatively. I was able to go out in shorts only, barefoot, with my dogs before sunrise, then tai chi in the backyard. Cloudy overcast, but the temps were in the upper 40s. This morning's cold shower was a bit tougher psychologically. I sense a resistance to it. I need to be careful of this signal. CT is a stressor and I've had lots of those in my life. Titrating the dosage will be key.
Reading Paleo Answer. Still have issues with glycemic index and loading. The statement that potatoes are worse than sugar does not hold with reality. I think the index is, at best, a first order approximation of reality. Though there is lots of good information in the book, it is still very bio_chemical_, ie not biophysics. In a certain sense, the Chinese are way ahead of us in interpreting reality energetically. We're still mostly stuck in the good/bad paradigm. We cannot use five phase theory as it stands either, but it would behoove us, imo of course, to learn from the approach and adapt the energetic viewpoint to our own needs. Again imo, quantum biology is one example.

Cold winds yesterday, did the dog walk and tai chi. Couldn't take a shower immediately after, so ate breakfast. Then took a cold shower. It was much more uncomfortable. Energy was calm and clear during the day, which was really nice because it was a very difficult day. Major upheavals in the lives of several people who are very close to me.

Today, 2of, winds out of the north, wind chill in single digits. Walked the dogs in a long sleeve t-shirt, gloves, hat, moccasins around sunrise. Very overcast so no direct sun. Did 25 mins of tai chi, then took a cold shower. The cold shower was much easier to tolerate. I had to check the faucet setting several times to be sure it was on all the way cold. I will probably try cold bath tomorrow.

Breakfast - 5 eggs from a local farm (still beautiful orange yolks) 1/5lb biodynamically-raised ground pork, large glass of kombucha, 1/2 glass of pu erh tea. Big glass of 'structured' cold water. Feel great this morning!

### Jack Kruse — 2017-12-10T19:37:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/michael-leger-opti-journal.20324/post-229953>

**Today’s precursor lesson for the Q & A tonight:** Today’s new data support the old conclusion that photobiomodulation is more pronounced in ill or otherwise stressed cells, as compared with healthy cells with plenty of oxygen available. The terminal enzyme of the mitochondrial respiratory chain and its electronic excitation by light with proper parameters causes retrograde light-sensitive cellular signaling events to transport the light signal from mitochondria to the nucleus to cause gene expression. The gene expression events caused by irradiation have been confirmed by experiment, and have been studied in more detail in recent years. The H in NADH must be generated in the TCA cycle and must be non deuterated. In all mitochondrial disease with high heteroplasmy NAD+ is low and there is a relative pseudohypoxia that leads to swelling of the nuclear membrane. This is where the December 2017 hammers the point home of why a broken TCA cycle of proton recycling leads to low NAD+ and pseudohypoxia.

(Sinclair’s Dec 2013 paper [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4076149/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4076149/)

I am able to handle the cold weather much more easily. ### Michael Leger — 2018-01-04T11:36:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/michael-leger-opti-journal.20324/post-231560>

I added red and infrared lights exposure earlier this week. I started with 15 mins of infrared and 660 nm LED before sunrise. I didn't sleep well that night. Down to 10 mins., still slept poorly. 5 mins, still poor sleep. 5 mins of infrared only, still poor sleep. I'll remove the lights from my routine for now. Oddly, it also seems to make the air CT harder. Don't know why yet, just noting that I am less inclined to do it and when I do, it is much harder physically and mentally. Wind chills are now regularly in the -20s f. This is the temp, at least for me, where things get tricky; frostbite is a real thing for ears, fingertips, toes and nose. I can cover the first three pretty easily, the nose is more difficult.
Breakfasts have generally been dinner for breakfast. I had 3/4 lb of sardines yesterday with 1/2 sweet potato, broccoli and parsley. During the work week, I go through the food more quickly, needing lunch. During weekends, 2 meals is typically sufficient now.

### Michael Leger — 2018-01-07T20:04:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/michael-leger-opti-journal.20324/post-231896>

I've eliminated the red/IR lights in the morning. Even 5 mins. of the IRA lamp is too stimulating. Something stays stimulated throughout the day and causes a restless night. Don't know what yet. Any thoughts would be appreciated.
Did a long sauna yesterday. I was concerned that maybe my ongoing cold adaptation would increase sensitivity to high temps. It does not appear to have done that. I tolerated the sauna very easily. I made naked snow angels twice during the sauna, then took a cold shower afterwards. Very refreshing.
Breakfast this morning was a berry smoothie with lots of spagyric heart-oriented herbs (_Sun_day is the day of the heart). I add psyllium for fiber, lots of coconut oil, cacao, kombucha and hemp milk, with 1/2lb of salmon. Shoveled snow in a tshirt and sweats.

### Michael Leger — 2018-02-04T21:28:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/michael-leger-opti-journal.20324/post-234134>

Much has changed since my last post. I have added, re-added really but it's been a while) ayurvedic herbs and practices a la John Douillard. I find the ayurvedic principle that we should be able to digest anything extremely appealing, and find it much healthier than the heavily restricted paleo diets. Our digestions are very poor and elimination is weak. Evidence of this assessment is abundant on this site, as well as other paleo, gluten-free, etc places.

### Michael Leger — 2018-02-08T17:03:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/michael-leger-opti-journal.20324/post-234357>

I think the strengths of this site are the emphasis on cleaning up exposure to nnEMF and Cold thermogenesis.

I am finding that being careful about exposure to blue light after sundown is somewhat helpful. I use sensation as my guideline. After wearing blue blocker glasses for a few months, I notice how 'harsh' light is that contains blue at night. I am using a 20 gauss magnetico with 5 gauss added diy pad. I do not notice any improvements in my sleep from it, but I do notice a fairly subtle improvement in feeling of wellness. Is it worth $2k? After two months, it's hard to say.

I am consistently doing some type of cold thermo. Today, I did Air CT, temp 8f, 20 mins of tai chi, then laid down in the snow for 6 minutes, 4 on my back, two on my front. I also walked my dogs dressed in sweat pants and a long sleeve shirt.

After 6 weeks or so of epi-paleo, I am reintegrating foods like potatoes, some beans, some grains. One of the biggest weaknesses of epi-paleo, imo of course, is the lack of fiber. I have also added some ayurvedic herbs to my regular regimen. I am finding that they are a bit too 'hot' energetically. Ashwagandha is a very interesting adaptogen.

**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2017-10-24T20:36:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-rarely-will-i-ask-you-to-act-but-i-want-to-see.20326/post-226504>

many of you comment here. I just went nuclear on this article. We need to make the comments VIRAL.

[https://www.buzzfeed.com/tomchivers...s-been-accused?utm\_term=.tgAWeaO8W#.xf94lqve4](https://www.buzzfeed.com/tomchivers/a-study-claiming-wifi-is-linked-to-autism-has-been-accused?utm_term=.tgAWeaO8W#.xf94lqve4)

### Lahelada — 2017-10-25T00:49:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-rarely-will-i-ask-you-to-act-but-i-want-to-see.20326/post-226512>

### Jake Tassell — 2017-10-25T01:35:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-rarely-will-i-ask-you-to-act-but-i-want-to-see.20326/post-226518>

5G wi fi has made it's entree in London. As they don't quite yet have the infrastructure for it, it seems the likes of Virgin Media are turning peoples apartments into 5G transmitters on the sly, in order to 'bump up the signal' in the street..

This from a contributor from another forum (back in August)....

**We recently had Virgin media installed in our house. Our office is on the 3rd floor and we have the livingroom on the ground floor. Naturally, we didn't want wifi in the house so we intended to have ethernet cables to supply our laptops in both the office and livingroom.

When the Virgin installation team came to set up the package the router was placed in the office with something called a powerline adapter which boosts the internet signal I believe. We told the installation guys that we didn't want wifi in the house so he gave us a powerline adapter for the office without wifi (it has an ethernet port). We also asked them to put a powerline adapter into the living room so we could connect to the internet down there as well. He went off to look in his van for another adapter and installed that as well.

It was explained to us that we would have 2 network log-ins, one for the office and one for downstairs. As all routers come with wifi we were just going to log into our router and turn off the wifi manually and use ethernet for everything. Now, when the guys left and we turned off the wifi my phone picked up a signal from the second powerline adapter in the livingroom despite us having disabled wifi it on the computer.

As it turns out, Virgin installed a new "test" powerline adapter with 5G wifi which looks exactly the same as the one without wifi as part of a trial for new customers in the area. (The old wifi powerline adapters have big bulky ariels on them so it is clear that they are emiting a signal) It was intended to boost the wifi signal of not only us but of our neighbours as well to be able to give better service in the area. When this second adapter is installed the customer doesn't have the ability to control the wifi from the router. This was only discovered after a lengthy phone conversation with Virgin who initially tried to deny that the adapter was emitting a wifi signal.

Virgin then tried to have us pay £40 to have a non-wifi adapter sent out to us because we were "privilaged" to be a part of this test even though we explicitly stated that we didn't want wifi in the house and that we had signed the worksheet to say that we were happy with the installation. I explained that we were being used as a part of a 5G trial without our knowledge and that I wasn't going to pay for a new adapter as I had signed the worksheet under the impression the the identical adapter was also non-wifi.

I was put on hold on the telephone for some time and while I was on hold my partner asked if I had taken the name of the person I was talking to. I hadn't, but when the man came back on the line we entered another discussion and then offered his name. So it seems that even when a customer is on hold they can still hear what is being said even though we can't hear them. Again I was put on hold and while I was on hold I said to my partner that we were still within the 14 day cancellation time and that we would just cancel the entire package and then re-sign up under his name with the 2 non-wifi adapters this time. I asked aloud how much that would cost Virgin compared to just sending the new adapter.

Suddenly I was back off hold and they were being nice as pie, they granted us the new wifi-free adapter and it would be getting fitted the very next day with no charge.

So, if you are having Virgin media installed please be aware of this 5G trial that may be put into your home without your permission in order to boost their wifi signal in the area. Also be aware that you can't control the wifi settings on the adapters so even if you turn the wifi off your router, the signal will still be getting sent from the adapters.**

### Michalis — 2017-10-25T13:26:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-rarely-will-i-ask-you-to-act-but-i-want-to-see.20326/post-226539>

I find it really hard to talk to people about it because i keep getting mocked about my tin-foil hat at work and i actually work in IT...I have this feeling that quite a few people usually take other people's words as BS by default. And i think that stems from the fact that nowadays people talk just to talk. The message doesn't matter , just only that you communicate... And that it is the wireless devices fault as well that have become extension of people's arms.. I dont think that it is only a quantum biology issue what is happening. It is a social issue. My opinion is that these two are in constant discourse. They feed each other...

I had to sent the oscillatorium link to my infrastructure director as he recons that we need to beef up the WiFi signal all around the building as we are running out of ethernet floor ports.. I also had to refuse a smart meter in the house from my electricity company.. I met some resistance but eventually they accepted the fact that i dont want it...
Maybe there is light in the tunnel maybe not.. I suppose we need to keep talking to people even if they think we are talking BS...

### Lahelada — 2017-10-25T16:01:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-rarely-will-i-ask-you-to-act-but-i-want-to-see.20326/post-226543>

Michalis, I am a teacher to adults. At some point they all get the talk ... usually when we hit a larger than usual concentration problem. One of my students is a very clever guy and he scoffed at me. I took an hour out f his time explaining the brain as best I could talking about nucleus accumbens,attention span ,dopamine etc and how lack of dopamine makes people available for any hit they can get from anything. No sale . Months later he said to me he had seen something on the internet that made him believe that we are getting less discerning in our choices. A seed of doubt is what you need to plant.

### Jake Tassell — 2017-10-25T19:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-rarely-will-i-ask-you-to-act-but-i-want-to-see.20326/post-226557>

You work in the IT Dept? Phew. Nuff tuff call. Turkeys voting for Xmas, etc.

In my workplace, years ago I was instrumental in setting up a 'No Cellphones' policy, on the sly proviso that it compromises productivity.

I don't worry too much about all that 'weirdo' stuff, because...

A) They knew that about me already.

B) Half the people that work there are internet conspiracy theorists of one sort or another, so it's quite normal in this environment for a supervisor to say...

"Can you put your Agenda 21 phone away please."

Of course, a lot of this kind of stuff is dependent on whether you have the personality to pull it off, bearing in mind that your bodily survival demands you keep your job and pay your rent.

### Jake Tassell — 2017-10-25T19:48:04+0300

The deal seems to be, what ever info you throw at them, a ton weight of disinfo comes back.
